title: West Bank

infobox.value: * Claimed by [Palestine](/wiki/Palestine "Palestine")[[a]](#cite_note-1)[[1]](#cite_note-2) * Partially [administered](/wiki/Palestinian_government "Palestinian government") by the [Palestinian National Authority](/wiki/Palestinian_National_Authority "Palestinian National Authority") in [Areas A and B](/wiki/Palestinian_enclaves "Palestinian enclaves")[[2]](#cite_note-3) * Under [Israeli occupation](/wiki/Israeli_occupation_of_the_West_Bank "Israeli occupation of the West Bank"), which is illegal under [international law](/wiki/International_law "International law")[[3]](#cite_note-bbc.com-4)[[4]](#cite_note-auto3-5)
[UTC](/wiki/Coordinated_Universal_Time "Coordinated Universal Time")+2 ([Palestine Standard Time](/wiki/Palestine_Standard_Time "Palestine Standard Time"))
[UTC](/wiki/Coordinated_Universal_Time "Coordinated Universal Time")+3 ([Palestine Summer Time](/wiki/Palestine_Summer_Time "Palestine Summer Time"))

infobox.key: Part of [a series](/wiki/Category:History_of_Palestine_(region) "Category:History of Palestine (region)") on the

infobox.value: is on the western bank of the [Jordan River](/wiki/Jordan_River "Jordan River") and is the larger of the two Palestinian territories (the other being the [Gaza Strip](/wiki/Gaza_Strip "Gaza Strip")) that make up the [State of Palestine](/wiki/State_of_Palestine "State of Palestine"). A landlocked territory near the coast of the [Mediterranean Sea](/wiki/Mediterranean_Sea "Mediterranean Sea") in [West Asia](/wiki/West_Asia "West Asia")'s [Levant](/wiki/Levant "Levant") region,[[7]](#cite_note-9) it is bordered by [Jordan](/wiki/Jordan "Jordan") and the [Dead Sea](/wiki/Dead_Sea "Dead Sea") to the east, and by [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel") (via the [Green Line](/wiki/Green_Line_(Israel) "Green Line (Israel)")) to the south, west, and north.[[8]](#cite_note-CIA-10) Since 1967, the territory has been under [Israeli occupation](/wiki/Israeli_occupation_of_the_West_Bank "Israeli occupation of the West Bank"), which has been [regarded as illegal under the law of the international community](/wiki/Legality_of_the_Israeli_occupation_of_Palestine "Legality of the Israeli occupation of Palestine").[[3]](#cite_note-bbc.com-4)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Palestinian territory occupied by Israel

This article is about the geographic region. For other uses, see [West Bank (disambiguation)](/wiki/West_Bank_(disambiguation) "West Bank (disambiguation)").

The **West Bank** is on the western bank of the [Jordan River](/wiki/Jordan_River "Jordan River") and is the larger of the two Palestinian territories (the other being the [Gaza Strip](/wiki/Gaza_Strip "Gaza Strip")) that make up the [State of Palestine](/wiki/State_of_Palestine "State of Palestine"). A landlocked territory near the coast of the [Mediterranean Sea](/wiki/Mediterranean_Sea "Mediterranean Sea") in [West Asia](/wiki/West_Asia "West Asia")'s [Levant](/wiki/Levant "Levant") region,[[7]](#cite_note-9) it is bordered by [Jordan](/wiki/Jordan "Jordan") and the [Dead Sea](/wiki/Dead_Sea "Dead Sea") to the east, and by [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel") (via the [Green Line](/wiki/Green_Line_(Israel) "Green Line (Israel)")) to the south, west, and north.[[8]](#cite_note-CIA-10) Since 1967, the territory has been under [Israeli occupation](/wiki/Israeli_occupation_of_the_West_Bank "Israeli occupation of the West Bank"), which has been [regarded as illegal under the law of the international community](/wiki/Legality_of_the_Israeli_occupation_of_Palestine "Legality of the Israeli occupation of Palestine").[[3]](#cite_note-bbc.com-4)

The territory first emerged in the wake of the [1948 Arab–Israeli War](/wiki/1948_Arab%E2%80%93Israeli_War "1948 Arab–Israeli War") as a region occupied and subsequently [annexed](/wiki/Jordanian_annexation_of_the_West_Bank "Jordanian annexation of the West Bank") by Jordan. Jordan ruled the territory until the 1967 [Six-Day War](/wiki/Six-Day_War "Six-Day War"), when it was occupied by Israel. Since then, [Israel has administered](/wiki/Israeli_Civil_Administration "Israeli Civil Administration") the West Bank (except for [East Jerusalem](/wiki/East_Jerusalem "East Jerusalem"), which was effectively annexed in 1980) as the [Judea and Samaria Area](/wiki/Judea_and_Samaria_Area "Judea and Samaria Area"). Jordan continued to claim the territory as its own until 1988. The mid-1990s [Oslo Accords](/wiki/Oslo_Accords "Oslo Accords") split the West Bank into [three regional levels](/wiki/West_Bank_Areas_in_the_Oslo_II_Accord "West Bank Areas in the Oslo II Accord") of Palestinian sovereignty, via the [Palestinian National Authority](/wiki/Palestinian_National_Authority "Palestinian National Authority") (PNA): Area A (PNA), Area B (PNA and Israel), and [Area C](/wiki/Area_C "Area C") (Israel, comprising 60% of the West Bank). The PNA exercises total or partial civil administration over 165 [Palestinian enclaves](/wiki/Palestinian_enclaves "Palestinian enclaves") across the three areas.

The West Bank remains central to the [Israeli–Palestinian conflict](/wiki/Israeli%E2%80%93Palestinian_conflict "Israeli–Palestinian conflict"). The [Palestinians](/wiki/Palestinians "Palestinians") consider it the heart of their envisaged state, along with the Gaza Strip. Right-wing and ideological[[9]](#cite_note-11)[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] Israelis see it as their [ancestral homeland](/wiki/History_of_ancient_Israel_and_Judah "History of ancient Israel and Judah"), with numerous biblical sites. There is a push among some Israelis for [partial or complete annexation](/wiki/Proposed_Israeli_annexation_of_the_West_Bank "Proposed Israeli annexation of the West Bank") of this land. Additionally, it is home to a rising number of Israeli settlers.[[10]](#cite_note-12) Area C contains 230 [Israeli settlements](/wiki/Israeli_settlement "Israeli settlement") where [Israeli law is applied](/wiki/Israeli_law_in_the_West_Bank_settlements "Israeli law in the West Bank settlements"). Under the Oslo Accords this area was to be mostly transferred to the PNA by 1997, but that did not occur.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWorld_Bank2013vii-13) The international community considers Israeli settlements in the West Bank to be illegal under [international law](/wiki/International_law "International law").[[4]](#cite_note-auto3-5)[[12]](#cite_note-maj2-14)[[13]](#cite_note-15)[[14]](#cite_note-16) Citing the 1980 law in which Israel claimed Jerusalem as its capital, the 1994 [Israel–Jordan peace treaty](/wiki/Israel%E2%80%93Jordan_peace_treaty "Israel–Jordan peace treaty"), and the Oslo Accords, a 2004 advisory ruling by the [International Court of Justice](/wiki/International_Court_of_Justice "International Court of Justice") (ICJ) concluded that the West Bank, including East Jerusalem, remains [Israeli-occupied territory](/wiki/Israeli-occupied_territory "Israeli-occupied territory").[[15]](#cite_note-FD2-17) In 2024 the ICJ again ruled that Israel's occupation of the West Bank is unlawful, adding that its conduct also violates the international prohibition on [racial segregation](/wiki/Racial_segregation "Racial segregation") and [apartheid](/wiki/Israeli_apartheid "Israeli apartheid").[[16]](#cite_note-:1-18)

The West Bank has a land area of about 5,640 square kilometres (2,180 square miles). It has an estimated population of 2,747,943 Palestinians and over 670,000 Israeli settlers, of which approximately 220,000 live in East Jerusalem.

## Etymology

### West Bank

The name "West Bank" is a translation of the Arabic term *aḍ-Ḍiffah al-Ġarbiyyah* ([Arabic](/wiki/Arabic_language "Arabic language"): الضفة الغربية), which designates the territory situated on the western side of the [Jordan River](/wiki/Jordan_River "Jordan River") that was [occupied in 1948](/wiki/1948_Arab%E2%80%93Israeli_War "1948 Arab–Israeli War") and [annexed in 1950](/wiki/Jordanian_annexation_of_the_West_Bank "Jordanian annexation of the West Bank") by the [Hashemite Kingdom of Jordan](/wiki/Jordan "Jordan"). This annexation was widely considered to be illegal, and was recognized only by [Iraq](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Iraq "Kingdom of Iraq"), [Pakistan](/wiki/Dominion_of_Pakistan "Dominion of Pakistan"), and the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom").[[17]](#cite_note-19) This term is also used in Hebrew ([Hebrew](/wiki/Hebrew_language "Hebrew language"): הַגָּדָה הַמַּעֲרָבִית, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Hebrew "Romanization of Hebrew"): *Hagada HaMa'aravit*), although the territory is more often referred to in Israel as [Judea and Samaria](/wiki/Judea_and_Samaria "Judea and Samaria") (Hebrew: יְהוּדָה וְשׁוֹמְרוֹן, romanized: *Yehuda VeShomron*).

### Cisjordan

Variations on the neo-[Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") name *Cisiordania* (lit. 'on this side of the River Jordan') are the usual names for the territory in [Romance languages](/wiki/Romance_languages "Romance languages"), e.g. [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"): *Cisjordania*, [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language"): *Cisjordânia*, [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *Cisjordanie*, [Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language"): *Cisgiordania*, and [Romanian](/wiki/Romanian_language "Romanian language"): *Cisiordania*, and in some others, e.g. [Basque](/wiki/Basque_language "Basque language"): *Zisjordania*, [Breton](/wiki/Breton_language "Breton language"): *Sisjordania*, [Hungarian](/wiki/Hungarian_language "Hungarian language"): *Ciszjordánia*, [Guarani](/wiki/Guarani_language "Guarani language"): *Sihorytáña*, and [Esperanto](/wiki/Esperanto_language "Esperanto language"): *Cisjordanio*.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The name of *Cisjordan* is also used by researchers and sometimes refers to the historical region of [Palestine](/wiki/Palestine_(region) "Palestine (region)").[[18]](#cite_note-20)[[19]](#cite_note-21)[[20]](#cite_note-22)

The name *West Bank*, however, has become the standard usage for this geopolitical entity in [English](/wiki/English_language "English language") and some of the other [Germanic languages](/wiki/Germanic_languages "Germanic languages") since its inception following the 1948 Jordanian capture.

The analogous *[Transjordan](/wiki/Transjordan_(region) "Transjordan (region)")* (lit. 'over the River Jordan') was historically used to designate the region now roughly comprising the Hashemite Kingdom of Jordan, which lies to the east of the Jordan River.

From 1517 to 1917, the area now known as the West Bank was under [Turkish rule](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire"), as part of [Ottoman Syria](/wiki/Ottoman_Syria "Ottoman Syria"). In the early Ottoman period, much of the northern West Bank, including the area of Jenin, and the hill country north of Nablus, formed part of the [Turabay Emirate](/wiki/Turabay_dynasty "Turabay dynasty"), a semi-autonomous Bedouin polity centred at [Lajjun](/wiki/Lajjun "Lajjun"). The Turabay clan administered wide territories on behalf of the Ottoman sultans from the sixteenth to the seventeenth century, collecting taxes and overseeing security along key trade routes. Their authority reflected the empire's reliance on local Arab elites to govern frontier districts, and the town of Lajjun became a regional administrative hub before its decline in the late seventeenth century.[[21]](#cite_note-23)

In the [late Ottoman period](/wiki/Late_Ottoman_period "Late Ottoman period"), the rural population of Palestine, including the West Bank hill country, expanded significantly. Villages grew in size, new hamlets were established, and peasants extended cultivation into previously marginal lands such as forests, marshes, and stony slopes. This process was part of a wider demographic recovery and agrarian commercialization in the region, which saw Palestinian peasants increasing grain production and integrating more fully into regional and international markets.[[22]](#cite_note-24)[[23]](#cite_note-25)[[24]](#cite_note-26)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/Cave_of_the_Patriarchs8.jpg/330px-Cave_of_the_Patriarchs8.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cave_of_the_Patriarchs8.jpg)

The [Cave of the Patriarchs](/wiki/Cave_of_the_Patriarchs "Cave of the Patriarchs") is one of the most famous holy sites in the region.

At the 1920 [San Remo conference](/wiki/San_Remo_conference "San Remo conference"), the victorious [Allies of World War I](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_I "Allies of World War I") allocated the area to the [British Mandate of Palestine](/wiki/Mandatory_Palestine "Mandatory Palestine") (1920–1948). The San Remo Resolution, adopted on 25 April 1920, incorporated the [Balfour Declaration](/wiki/Balfour_Declaration "Balfour Declaration") of 1917. It and Article 22 of the Covenant of the [League of Nations](/wiki/League_of_Nations "League of Nations") were the basic documents upon which the British Mandate of Palestine was constructed. The [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland") proclaimed [Abdullah I](/wiki/Abdullah_I_of_Jordan "Abdullah I of Jordan") as [emir](/wiki/Emir "Emir") of the [Emirate of Transjordan](/wiki/Emirate_of_Transjordan "Emirate of Transjordan") on 11 April 1921. He declared it an independent [Hashemite](/wiki/Hashemite "Hashemite") kingdom on 25 May 1946.

Under the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") in 1947, it was designated as part of a proposed [Arab state](/wiki/Arab_state "Arab state") by the [Partition Plan for Palestine](/wiki/United_Nations_Partition_Plan_for_Palestine "United Nations Partition Plan for Palestine"). UN Resolution 181 recommended the splitting of the British Mandate into a [Jewish state](/wiki/Jewish_state "Jewish state"), an Arab state, and an [internationally administered enclave of Jerusalem](/wiki/Corpus_separatum_(Jerusalem) "Corpus separatum (Jerusalem)").[[25]](#cite_note-27) A broader region of the modern-day West Bank was assigned to the Arab state. The resolution designated the territory described as "the hill country of [Samaria](/wiki/Samaria "Samaria") and [Judea](/wiki/Judea "Judea")",[[26]](#cite_note-28) the area now known as the "West Bank", as part of the proposed Arab state. Following the [1948 Arab–Israeli War](/wiki/1948_Arab%E2%80%93Israeli_War "1948 Arab–Israeli War"), this area was captured by [Transjordan](/wiki/Jordan "Jordan").[[27]](#cite_note-29)

### 1948–1967: Jordanian West Bank

Further information: [Jordanian annexation of the West Bank](/wiki/Jordanian_annexation_of_the_West_Bank "Jordanian annexation of the West Bank"), [King Hussein's federation plan](/wiki/King_Hussein%27s_federation_plan "King Hussein's federation plan"), [Islamization of Jerusalem](/wiki/Islamization_of_Jerusalem "Islamization of Jerusalem"), and [Palestinians in Jordan](/wiki/Palestinians_in_Jordan "Palestinians in Jordan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/King_Hussein_flying_over_Temple_Mount_when_it_was_under_Jordanian_control.jpg/330px-King_Hussein_flying_over_Temple_Mount_when_it_was_under_Jordanian_control.jpg)](/wiki/File:King_Hussein_flying_over_Temple_Mount_when_it_was_under_Jordanian_control.jpg)

[King Hussein](/wiki/Hussein_of_Jordan "Hussein of Jordan") flying over the [Temple Mount](/wiki/Temple_Mount "Temple Mount") in Jerusalem when it was under Jordanian control, 1965

During the 1948 war, Israel occupied parts of what was designated in the UN partition plan as "Palestine". The [1949 Armistice Agreements](/wiki/1949_Armistice_Agreements "1949 Armistice Agreements") defined the [interim boundary](/wiki/Green_Line_(Israel) "Green Line (Israel)") between Israel and Jordan, essentially reflecting the battlefield after the war.[[28]](#cite_note-JordanIsraelArmistice1949-30) Following the December 1948 [Jericho Conference](/wiki/Jericho_Conference "Jericho Conference"), Transjordan annexed the area west of the Jordan River in 1950, naming it "West Bank" or "Cisjordan", and designated the area east of the river as "East Bank" or "Transjordan". Jordan, as it was now known, ruled over the West Bank from 1948 until 1967. Jordan's annexation was never formally recognized by the international community, with the exception of the United Kingdom and Iraq.[[29]](#cite_note-digicoll.library.wisc.edu-31)[[30]](#cite_note-Silverburg-32)[[31]](#cite_note-33) [King Abdullah of Jordan](/wiki/Abdullah_I_of_Jordan "Abdullah I of Jordan") was crowned King of Jerusalem by the Coptic Bishop on 15 November 1948.[[32]](#cite_note-34) Palestinian Arabs in the West Bank, including [East Jerusalem](/wiki/East_Jerusalem "East Jerusalem"), were granted Jordanian citizenship and half of the [Jordanian Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_Jordan "Parliament of Jordan") seats, thus enjoying equal opportunities in all sectors of the state.[[33]](#cite_note-35)[[34]](#cite_note-jifa-36)

Many refugees continued to live in camps and relied on [UNRWA](/wiki/UNRWA "UNRWA") assistance for sustenance. Palestinian refugees constituted more than a third of the kingdom's population of 1.5 million. The last Jordanian elections in which West Bank residents voted were those of April 1967. Their parliamentary representatives continued in office until 1988, when West Bank seats were abolished. Palestinians enjoyed equal opportunities in all sectors of the state without discrimination.[[34]](#cite_note-jifa-36) Agriculture remained the primary activity of the territory. The West Bank, despite its smaller area, contained half of Jordan's agricultural land.[[35]](#cite_note-auto2-37)

In 1966, 43% of the labour force of 55,000 worked in agriculture, and 2,300 km2 were under cultivation. In 1965, 15,000 workers were employed in industry, producing 7% of the GNP. This number fell after the 1967 war and was not surpassed until 1983.[[35]](#cite_note-auto2-37) The [tourism](/wiki/Tourism "Tourism") industry played an important role. 26 branches of 8 Arab banks were present. The [Jordanian dinar](/wiki/Jordanian_dinar "Jordanian dinar") became legal tender and remains so until today.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] 80% of Jordan's fruit-growing land and 40% of its vegetables lay in the West Bank. With the onset of the occupation, the area could no longer produce export earnings.[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECooley198413-38)

On the eve of occupation, the West Bank accounted for 40% of Jordanian GNP, between 34% and 40% of its agricultural output, and almost half of its manpower, though only a third of Jordanian investment was allocated to it and mainly to the private housing construction sector.[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMansour201573–74-39) Even though its per-capita product was 10 times greater than that of the West Bank, the Israeli economy on the eve of occupation had experienced two years (1966–1967) of a sharp recession.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUnctad20165-40)

Immediately after the occupation, from 1967 to 1974, the economy boomed. In 1967, the Palestinian economy had a gross domestic product of $1,349 per capita for a million people.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUnctad20165-40) The West Bank's population was 585,500,[[c]](#cite_note-42) of whom 18% were refugees, and was growing annually by 2%. West Bank growth, compared to Gaza (3%), had lagged, due to the effect of mass emigration of West Bankers seeking employment in Jordan.[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETumaDarin-Drabkin197847,_50-43) As agriculture gave way to industrial development in Israel, in the West Bank the former still generated 37% of domestic product, and industry a mere 13%.[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVan_Arkadie1977104-44)

The growth rate of the West Bank economy in the period of the Jordanian rule of the West Bank, before Israeli occupation, had risen at an annual rate of 6–8%. This rate of growth was indispensable if the post-war West Bank were to achieve [economic self-reliance](/wiki/Self-sustainability "Self-sustainability").

### 1967–present: Israeli Military Governorate and Civil Administration

Main articles: [Israeli Military Governorate](/wiki/Israeli_Military_Governorate "Israeli Military Governorate") and [Israeli Civil Administration](/wiki/Israeli_Civil_Administration "Israeli Civil Administration")

In June 1967, the West Bank and East Jerusalem were captured by Israel as a result of the [Six-Day War](/wiki/Six-Day_War "Six-Day War"). With the exception of East Jerusalem and the former [Israeli–Jordanian no man's land](/wiki/No_man%27s_land_(West_Bank)#Israel–Jordan "No man's land (West Bank)"), the West Bank was not annexed by Israel. It remained under [Israeli military control](/wiki/Israeli_Military_Governorate "Israeli Military Governorate") until 1982.

The [1974 Arab League summit](/wiki/1974_Arab_League_summit "1974 Arab League summit") resolution at [Rabat](/wiki/Rabat "Rabat") designated the [Palestine Liberation Organization](/wiki/Palestine_Liberation_Organization "Palestine Liberation Organization") (PLO) as the "sole legitimate representative of the Palestinian people". Jordan did not officially relinquish its claim to the area until 1988,[[42]](#cite_note-45) when it severed all administrative and legal ties with the West Bank and eventually stripped West Bank Palestinians of Jordanian citizenship.[[43]](#cite_note-46)

In 1982, as a result of the [Egypt–Israel peace treaty](/wiki/Egypt%E2%80%93Israel_peace_treaty "Egypt–Israel peace treaty"), the direct military rule was transformed into a [semi-civil authority](/wiki/Israeli_Civil_Administration "Israeli Civil Administration"), operating directly under the Israeli Ministry of Defense, taking control of civil matters of Palestinians from the IDF to civil servants in the Ministry of Defense. The Israeli settlements were administered as [Judea and Samaria Area](/wiki/Judea_and_Samaria_Area "Judea and Samaria Area"), directly by Israel.

Since the 1993 [Oslo Accords](/wiki/Oslo_Accords "Oslo Accords"), the [Palestinian Authority](/wiki/Palestinian_Authority "Palestinian Authority") officially controls a geographically non-contiguous territory comprising approximately 11% of the West Bank, known as [Area A](/wiki/Palestinian_enclaves "Palestinian enclaves"), which remains subject to Israeli incursions. Area B, approximately 28%, is subject to joint Israeli-Palestinian military and Palestinian civil control. [Area C](/wiki/Area_C_(West_Bank) "Area C (West Bank)"), approximately 61%, is under full Israeli control. Though 164 nations refer to the West Bank, including East Jerusalem, as "[Occupied Palestinian Territory](/wiki/Israeli-occupied_territories "Israeli-occupied territories")",[[44]](#cite_note-UNGeneva_Convention-47)[[45]](#cite_note-ICRCGeneva_Convention-48) the State of Israel quotes the UN that only territories captured in war from "an established and recognized sovereign" are considered occupied territories.[[46]](#cite_note-GovILDisputed_Territories-49)

After the [2007 split](/wiki/Battle_of_Gaza_(2007) "Battle of Gaza (2007)") between [Fatah](/wiki/Fatah "Fatah") and [Hamas](/wiki/Hamas "Hamas"), the West Bank areas under Palestinian control are an exclusive part of the Palestinian Authority. The [Gaza Strip](/wiki/Gaza_Strip "Gaza Strip") is [ruled by Hamas](/wiki/Gaza_Strip_under_Hamas "Gaza Strip under Hamas").

## Geography

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/Judean_Hills_from_Ramallah.jpg/330px-Judean_Hills_from_Ramallah.jpg)](/wiki/File:Judean_Hills_from_Ramallah.jpg)

View of the [Judaean Mountains](/wiki/Judaean_Mountains "Judaean Mountains") from [Ramallah](/wiki/Ramallah "Ramallah")

The West Bank has an area of 5,628 or 5,640 square kilometres (2,173 or 2,178 square miles), which comprises 21.2% of former Mandatory Palestine (excluding Jordan)[[47]](#cite_note-Arnon-50) and has generally rugged mountainous terrain. The total length of the land boundaries of the region is 404 km (251 mi).[[8]](#cite_note-CIA-10) The terrain is mostly rugged dissected upland, some vegetation in the west, but somewhat barren in the east. The elevation span between the shoreline of the Dead Sea at −408 m to the highest point at [Mount Nabi Yunis](/wiki/Mount_Nabi_Yunis,_Palestine "Mount Nabi Yunis, Palestine"), at 1,030 m (3,379 ft) [above sea level](/wiki/Above_mean_sea_level "Above mean sea level").[[48]](#cite_note-51) The West Bank is landlocked; its highlands are the main recharge area for Israel's coastal aquifers.[[8]](#cite_note-CIA-10)

The West Bank has 220 km2 (85 sq mi) of water area, consisting of the northwestern quarter of the Dead Sea.

There are few natural resources in the area except the highly arable land, which comprises 27% of the land area of the region. It is mostly used as permanent pastures (32% of arable land) and seasonal agricultural uses (40%).[[8]](#cite_note-CIA-10) Forests and woodland comprise just 1%, with no permanent crops.[[8]](#cite_note-CIA-10)

### Climate

The climate in the West Bank is mostly [Mediterranean](/wiki/Mediterranean "Mediterranean"), slightly cooler at elevated areas compared with the shoreline, west to the area. In the east, the West Bank includes the [Judean Desert](/wiki/Judaean_Desert "Judaean Desert") and the shoreline of the Dead Sea – both with dry and hot climate.

In the most highly elevated areas in the northwest, annual rainfall is more than 27 inches (685 mm). This declines in the southwest and southeast to less than 4 inches (100 mm). Hilly terrains are used for sheep grazing and cultivating olives, cereals, and fruits. The Jordan River valley is also cultivated for various fruits and vegetables.[[49]](#cite_note-52)

#### Palestinian enclaves

Main articles: [Palestinian enclaves](/wiki/Palestinian_enclaves "Palestinian enclaves") and [Palestinian National Authority](/wiki/Palestinian_National_Authority "Palestinian National Authority")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0d/Settlements2006.jpg/330px-Settlements2006.jpg)](/wiki/File:Settlements2006.jpg)

A map of West Bank settlements and closures in January 2006: Yellow = Palestinian urban centres. Light pink = closed military areas or settlement boundary areas or areas isolated by the [Israeli West Bank barrier](/wiki/Israeli_West_Bank_barrier "Israeli West Bank barrier"); dark pink = settlements, outposts or military bases. The black line = route of the Barrier

The 1993 [Oslo Accords](/wiki/Oslo_Accords "Oslo Accords") declared the final status of the West Bank to be subject to a forthcoming settlement between [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel") and the Palestinian leadership. Following these interim accords, Israel withdrew its military rule from some parts of the West Bank, which was divided into three [Areas](/wiki/West_Bank_Areas_in_the_Oslo_II_Accord "West Bank Areas in the Oslo II Accord"):

| Area | Security | Civil Admin | % of WB land | % of WB Palestinians |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| A | Palestinian | Palestinian | 18% | 55% |
| B | Israeli | Palestinian | 21% | 41% |
| [C](/wiki/Area_C_(West_Bank) "Area C (West Bank)") | Israeli | Israeli | 61% | 4%[[50]](#cite_note-53) |

Area A, 2.7% of the West Bank under full civil control of the Palestinian Authority comprises Palestinian towns and some rural areas away from Israeli settlements in the north (between [Jenin](/wiki/Jenin "Jenin"), [Nablus](/wiki/Nablus "Nablus"), [Tubas](/wiki/Tubas_(city) "Tubas (city)"), and [Tulkarm](/wiki/Tulkarm "Tulkarm")), the south (around [Hebron](/wiki/Hebron "Hebron")), and one in the centre south of [Salfit](/wiki/Salfit "Salfit").[[51]](#cite_note-Oslo_2-54) Area B, 25.2% of the area of the terriroty, adds what was the contemporary populated rural areas, many closer to the centre of the West Bank. Area C contains all the [Israeli settlements](/wiki/Israeli_settlements "Israeli settlements") (excluding settlements in East Jerusalem), roads that afford access to the settlements, buffer zones (near settlements, roads, strategic areas, and Israel), and almost all of the [Jordan Valley](/wiki/Jordan_Valley "Jordan Valley") and the [Judean Desert](/wiki/Judean_Desert "Judean Desert").

Areas A and B are themselves divided among 227 separate areas (199 of which are smaller than 2 km2 (1 sq mi)) that are separated from one another by Israeli-controlled Area C.
[[52]](#cite_note-55) Areas A, B, and C cross the 11 [governorates](/wiki/Governorates_of_the_Palestinian_National_Authority "Governorates of the Palestinian National Authority") used as administrative divisions by the Palestinian National Authority, Israel, and the IDF and named after major cities. The mainly open areas of Area C, which contains all of the basic resources of arable and building land, water springs, quarries, and sites of touristic value needed to develop a viable Palestinian state,[[53]](#cite_note-56) were to be handed over to the Palestinians by 1999 under the Oslo Accords as part of a final status agreement. This agreement was never achieved.[[54]](#cite_note-57)

According to [B'tselem](/wiki/B%27tselem "B'tselem"), while the vast majority of the Palestinian population lives in areas A and B, the vacant land available for construction in dozens of villages and towns across the West Bank is situated on the margins of the communities and defined as area C.[[55]](#cite_note-58) Less than 1% of area C is designated for use by Palestinians, who are also unable to legally build in their own existing villages in area C due to Israeli authorities' restrictions.[[56]](#cite_note-AUS2922p4-59)[[57]](#cite_note-IllegalSettlements-C-60)

An assessment by the UN [Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs](/wiki/Office_for_the_Coordination_of_Humanitarian_Affairs "Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs") in 2007 found that approximately 40% of the West Bank was taken up by Israeli infrastructure. The infrastructure, consisting of settlements, the [barrier](/wiki/West_Bank_barrier "West Bank barrier"), military bases and closed military areas, Israeli-declared nature reserves, and the roads that accompany them is off-limits or tightly controlled to Palestinians.[[58]](#cite_note-OCHAoPtHumanitarianImpact-61)

In June 2011, the Independent Commission for Human Rights published a report that found that Palestinians in the West Bank and the [Gaza Strip](/wiki/Gaza_Strip "Gaza Strip") were subjected in 2010 to an "almost systematic campaign" of human rights abuse by the [Palestinian Authority](/wiki/Palestinian_Authority "Palestinian Authority") and [Hamas](/wiki/Hamas "Hamas"), as well as by [Israeli](/wiki/Israel "Israel") authorities, with the security forces of the PA and Hamas being responsible for torture, arrests, and arbitrary detentions.[[59]](#cite_note-62)

#### Area annexed by Israel

Main article: [East Jerusalem](/wiki/East_Jerusalem "East Jerusalem")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7c/Greater_Jerusalem_May_2006_CIA_remote-sensing_map_3500px.jpg/330px-Greater_Jerusalem_May_2006_CIA_remote-sensing_map_3500px.jpg)](/wiki/File:Greater_Jerusalem_May_2006_CIA_remote-sensing_map_3500px.jpg)

[Greater Jerusalem](/wiki/Greater_Jerusalem "Greater Jerusalem"), May 2006. [CIA](/wiki/CIA "CIA") [remote sensing](/wiki/Remote_sensing "Remote sensing") map showing areas considered settlements, plus refugee camps, fences, walls, etc.

Through the [Jerusalem Law](/wiki/Jerusalem_Law "Jerusalem Law"), Israel extended its administrative control over [East Jerusalem](/wiki/East_Jerusalem "East Jerusalem"). This has often been interpreted as tantamount to an official annexation, though [Ian Lustick](/wiki/Ian_Lustick "Ian Lustick"), in reviewing the legal status of Israeli measures, has argued that no such annexation ever took place. The Palestinian residents have legal [permanent residency](/wiki/Permanent_residency "Permanent residency") status.[[60]](#cite_note-63)[[61]](#cite_note-64) Rejecting the Jerusalem Law, the UN Security Council passed [UN Security Council Resolution 478](/wiki/UN_Security_Council_Resolution_478 "UN Security Council Resolution 478"), declaring that the law was "null and void". Although permanent residents are permitted, if they wish, to receive Israeli citizenship if they meet certain conditions including swearing allegiance to the State and renouncing any other citizenship, most Palestinians did not apply for Israeli citizenship for political reasons.[[62]](#cite_note-65) There are various possible reasons as to why the West Bank had not been annexed to Israel after its [capture in 1967](/wiki/Six-Day_War "Six-Day War").[[63]](#cite_note-MBard-66) The government of Israel has not formally confirmed an official reason; however, historians and analysts have established a variety of such, most of them demographic. Among those most commonly cited have been:

* Reluctance to award its citizenship to an overwhelming number of a potentially hostile population whose allies were sworn to the destruction of Israel.[[64]](#cite_note-Bard-67)[[65]](#cite_note-Bamberger-68)
* To ultimately exchange [land for peace](/wiki/Land_for_peace "Land for peace") with neighbouring states[[64]](#cite_note-Bard-67)[[65]](#cite_note-Bamberger-68)
* Fear that the population of ethnic Arabs, including Israeli citizens of Palestinian ethnicity, would outnumber the Jewish Israelis west of the Jordan River.[[63]](#cite_note-MBard-66)[[64]](#cite_note-Bard-67)
* The disputed legality of [annexation](/wiki/Annexation "Annexation") under the [Fourth Geneva Convention](/wiki/Fourth_Geneva_Convention "Fourth Geneva Convention")[[66]](#cite_note-69)

The importance of demographic concerns to some significant figures in Israel's leadership was illustrated when [Avraham Burg](/wiki/Avraham_Burg "Avraham Burg"), a former Knesset Speaker and former chair of the Jewish Agency for Israel, wrote in *The Guardian* in September 2003,

:   "Between the Jordan and the Mediterranean there is no longer a clear Jewish majority. And so, fellow citizens, it is not possible to keep the whole thing without paying a price. We cannot keep a Palestinian majority under an Israeli boot and at the same time think ourselves the only democracy in the Middle East. There cannot be democracy without equal rights for all who live here, Arab as well as Jew. We cannot keep the territories and preserve a Jewish majority in the world's only Jewish state – not by means that are humane and moral and Jewish."[[67]](#cite_note-70)

#### Area C and Israeli settlements

Main articles: [Area C (West Bank)](/wiki/Area_C_(West_Bank) "Area C (West Bank)") and [International law and Israeli settlements](/wiki/International_law_and_Israeli_settlements "International law and Israeli settlements")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4d/West_Bank_Access_Restrictions_%28United_Nations_OCHA_oPt%29_May_2023_01.pdf/page1-330px-West_Bank_Access_Restrictions_%28United_Nations_OCHA_oPt%29_May_2023_01.pdf.jpg)](/wiki/File:West_Bank_Access_Restrictions_(United_Nations_OCHA_oPt)_May_2023_01.pdf)

Map of [Israeli settlements](/wiki/Israeli_settlement "Israeli settlement") and [Area C](/wiki/Area_C_(West_Bank) "Area C (West Bank)") (magenta and blue), 2023

As of 2022, there are over 450,000 [Israeli settlers](/wiki/Israeli_settlement "Israeli settlement") living in 132 [Israeli settlements](/wiki/Israeli_settlement "Israeli settlement") in the West Bank excluding East Jerusalem, with an additional 220,000 Jewish settlers residing in 12 settlements in [East Jerusalem](/wiki/East_Jerusalem "East Jerusalem").[[68]](#cite_note-:52-71)[[69]](#cite_note-:42-72)[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] In addition, there are over 140 [Israeli outposts](/wiki/Israeli_outpost "Israeli outpost") in the West Bank that are not recognized and are therefore illegal even under Israeli law, but which have nevertheless been provided with infrastructure, water, sewage, and other services by the authorities. They are colloquially known as "[illegal outposts](/wiki/Israeli_outpost "Israeli outpost")".[[70]](#cite_note-btselem-73)

As a result of [the application of Israeli law in the settlements ("Enclave law")](/wiki/Israeli_law_in_the_West_Bank_settlements "Israeli law in the West Bank settlements"), large portions of Israeli [civil law](/wiki/Civil_law_(legal_system) "Civil law (legal system)") are applied to Israeli settlements and to Israelis living in the [Israeli-occupied territories](/wiki/Israeli-occupied_territories "Israeli-occupied territories").[[71]](#cite_note-Ben-NaftaliSfard2018-74)

The international consensus is that all Israeli settlements on the West Bank are illegal under international law.[[72]](#cite_note-75)[[73]](#cite_note-76)[[74]](#cite_note-77)[[75]](#cite_note-78) In 2002, the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") as a whole found all Israeli settlement activities to be illegal.[[76]](#cite_note-79) Significant portions of the Israeli public similarly oppose the continuing presence of Jewish Israelis in the West Bank and have supported the 2005 settlement relocation.[[77]](#cite_note-80) The majority of legal scholars hold the settlements to violate international law;[[12]](#cite_note-maj2-14) however, some others, including [Julius Stone](/wiki/Julius_Stone "Julius Stone"),[[78]](#cite_note-81)[[79]](#cite_note-82) [Eugene Rostow](/wiki/Eugene_Rostow "Eugene Rostow"),[[80]](#cite_note-83) and [Eugene Kontorovich](/wiki/Eugene_Kontorovich "Eugene Kontorovich"),[[81]](#cite_note-84) have argued that they are legal under international law.[[82]](#cite_note-85)

Immediately after the 1967 war, [Theodor Meron](/wiki/Theodor_Meron "Theodor Meron"), legal counsellor of Israel's Foreign Ministry, advised Israeli ministers in a "top secret" memo that any policy of building settlements across occupied territories violated international law and would "contravene the explicit provisions of the Fourth Geneva Convention".[[83]](#cite_note-86)[[84]](#cite_note-87)[[85]](#cite_note-88) Fifty years later, citing decades of legal scholarship on the subject, Meron reiterated his legal opinion regarding the illegality of Israeli settlements in the Occupied Territories.[[86]](#cite_note-89)

The UN Security Council has issued several non-binding resolutions addressing the issue of the settlements. Typical of these is UN Security Council resolution 446 which states that the "practices of Israel in establishing settlements in the Palestinian and other Arab territories occupied since 1967 have no legal validity", and it calls on Israel "as the occupying Power, to abide scrupulously by the [1949 Fourth Geneva Convention](/wiki/Fourth_Geneva_Convention "Fourth Geneva Convention")"*.*[[87]](#cite_note-90)

The Conference of High Contracting Parties to the Fourth Geneva Convention held in Geneva on 5 December 2001 called upon "the Occupying Power to fully and effectively respect the Fourth Geneva Convention in the Occupied Palestinian Territory, including East Jerusalem, and to refrain from perpetrating any violation of the Convention." The High Contracting Parties reaffirmed "the illegality of the settlements in the said territories and of the extension thereof."[[88]](#cite_note-91)

On 30 December 2007, [Israeli Prime Minister](/wiki/Israeli_Prime_Minister "Israeli Prime Minister") [Ehud Olmert](/wiki/Ehud_Olmert "Ehud Olmert") issued an order requiring approval by both the Israeli Prime Minister and Israeli Defense Minister of all settlement activities (including planning) in the West Bank.[[89]](#cite_note-92) The change had little effect with settlements continuing to expand, and new ones being established. On 31 August 2014, Israel announced it was appropriating 400 hectares of land in the West Bank to eventually house 1,000 Israel families. The appropriation was described as the largest in more than 30 years.[[90]](#cite_note-NewSettlement-93) According to reports on Israel Radio, the development is a response to the [2014 kidnapping and murder of Israeli teenagers](/wiki/2014_kidnapping_and_murder_of_Israeli_teenagers "2014 kidnapping and murder of Israeli teenagers").[[90]](#cite_note-NewSettlement-93)

in February 2026, the Israeli government approved a plan to designate lands in Area C of the West Bank as state property, aiming to strengthen administrative control over settlements and their surrounding regions.[[91]](#cite_note-94)[[92]](#cite_note-95) The initiative includes mapping both privately owned and state lands, introducing new regulatory systems, and incorporating municipal oversight to support infrastructure development and governance within the settlements.[[93]](#cite_note-96) Israel’s designation of specific regions within the West Bank as state property has generated extensive criticism, with allegations that this action contravenes established international legal standards, notably the Fourth Geneva Convention, which forbids changes to the status of occupied territories.[[94]](#cite_note-97) Palestinian authorities and human rights proponents regard this action as a de facto annexation that diminishes the viability of a two-state resolution. Legal scholars highlight that unilateral land registration and regulatory interventions in occupied territories contravene recognized international legal norms and may contribute to the intensification of regional tensions.[[95]](#cite_note-98)[[96]](#cite_note-99)

#### Palestinian outposts

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e3/Palestinian_demonstration_against_demolish_of_the_village_susya.jpg/330px-Palestinian_demonstration_against_demolish_of_the_village_susya.jpg)](/wiki/File:Palestinian_demonstration_against_demolish_of_the_village_susya.jpg)

A Palestinian demonstration against the demolition of the village [Susya](/wiki/Susya "Susya")

The [Haaretz](/wiki/Haaretz "Haaretz") newspaper published an article in December 2005 about demolition of "Palestinian outposts" in [Bil'in](/wiki/Bil%27in "Bil'in").[[97]](#cite_note-100) The demolitions sparked a political debate as according to *PeaceNow* it was a double standard ("After what happened today in Bil'in, there is no reason that the state should defend its decision to continue the construction" credited to [Michael Sfard](/wiki/Michael_Sfard "Michael Sfard")).

In January 2012, the European Union approved the "Area C and Palestinian state building" report. The report said Palestinian presence in Area C has been continuously undermined by Israel and that state building efforts in Area C of the Palestinian Authority (PA) and the EU were of "utmost importance in order to support the creation of a contiguous and viable Palestinian state". The EU will support various projects to "support the Palestinian people and help maintain their presence".[[98]](#cite_note-101)[[99]](#cite_note-jps41-3-102)

In May 2012, a petition[[100]](#cite_note-may8p-103) was filed to the [Israeli Supreme Court](/wiki/Israeli_Supreme_Court "Israeli Supreme Court") about the legality of more 15[[100]](#cite_note-may8p-103) Palestinian outposts and Palestinian building in "Area C". The cases were filed by [Regavim](/wiki/Regavim_(NGO) "Regavim (NGO)").[[101]](#cite_note-nrgheb-104)[[102]](#cite_note-105)

The petition was one of 30 different petitions with the common ground of illegal land takeover and illegal construction and use of natural resources. Some of the petitions (27) had been set for trials[[103]](#cite_note-list_of_pettions-106) and the majority received a verdict.

*Ynet News* stated on 11 January 2013 that a group of 200 Palestinians with unknown number of foreign activists created an outpost named [Bab al-Shams](/wiki/Bab_al-Shams "Bab al-Shams") ("Gate of the Sun"), contains 50 tents[[104]](#cite_note-107)

*Ynet News* stated on 18 January 2013 that Palestinian activists built an outpost on a disputed area in [Beit Iksa](/wiki/Beit_Iksa "Beit Iksa"), where Israel plans to construct part of the separation fence in the Jerusalem vicinity while the Palestinians claim that the area belongs to the residents of Beit Iksa. named Bab al-Krama.[[105]](#cite_note-108)

#### West Bank barrier

Main article: [West Bank barrier](/wiki/West_Bank_barrier "West Bank barrier")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0a/West_Bank_barrier.jpg/330px-West_Bank_barrier.jpg)](/wiki/File:West_Bank_barrier.jpg)

[West Bank barrier](/wiki/West_Bank_barrier "West Bank barrier")

The West Bank barrier is a physical [barrier](/wiki/Separation_barrier "Separation barrier") ordered for construction by the Israeli government, consisting of a network of fences with vehicle-barrier trenches. Israel began building the barrier on 23 June 2002,[[106]](#cite_note-109) two years into the [Second Intifada](/wiki/Second_Intifada "Second Intifada").[[107]](#cite_note-110) It is surrounded by an on average 60 m (197 ft) wide exclusion area (90%) and up to 8 m (26 ft) high concrete walls (10%), although in most areas the wall is not nearly that high.[[108]](#cite_note-111) It is located mainly within the West Bank, partly along the [1949 Armistice line](/wiki/1949_Armistice_Agreements "1949 Armistice Agreements"), or "[Green Line](/wiki/Green_Line_(Israel) "Green Line (Israel)")" between the West Bank and Israel. The length of the barrier as approved by the Israeli government is 708 km (440 mi) long.[[109]](#cite_note-:0-112) As of 2020, approximately 454 km (282 mi) have been constructed (64%).[[109]](#cite_note-:0-112)[[110]](#cite_note-113) The space between the barrier and the green line is a closed military zone known as the [Seam Zone](/wiki/Seam_Zone "Seam Zone"), cutting off 9% of the West Bank and encompassing dozens of villages and tens of thousands of Palestinians.[[111]](#cite_note-B-114)[[112]](#cite_note-CBC-115)[[113]](#cite_note-ICJ-116)

The barrier generally runs along or near the 1949 Jordanian-Israeli armistice/Green Line, but diverges in many places to include on the Israeli side several of the highly populated areas of Jewish settlements in the West Bank such as [East Jerusalem](/wiki/East_Jerusalem "East Jerusalem"), [Ariel](/wiki/Ariel_(city) "Ariel (city)"), [Gush Etzion](/wiki/Gush_Etzion "Gush Etzion"), [Immanuel](/wiki/Immanuel_(town) "Immanuel (town)"), [Karnei Shomron](/wiki/Karnei_Shomron "Karnei Shomron"), [Givat Ze'ev](/wiki/Givat_Ze%27ev "Givat Ze'ev"), [Oranit](/wiki/Oranit "Oranit"), and [Maale Adumim](/wiki/Maale_Adumim "Maale Adumim").

Supporters of the barrier stated it was necessary for protecting Israeli civilians from Palestinian attacks, which increased significantly during the [Al-Aqsa Intifada](/wiki/Second_Intifada "Second Intifada");[[114]](#cite_note-117)[[115]](#cite_note-118) it has helped reduce incidents of terrorism by 90% from 2002 to 2005; over a 96% reduction in terror attacks in the six years ending in 2007,[[116]](#cite_note-119) though Israel's State Comptroller has acknowledged that most of the suicide bombers crossed into Israel through existing checkpoints.[[117]](#cite_note-120) Its supporters state that the [onus](/wiki/Philosophic_burden_of_proof "Philosophic burden of proof") is now on the Palestinian Authority to fight terrorism.[[118]](#cite_note-121)

Opponents state the barrier is an illegal attempt to annex Palestinian land under the guise of security,[[119]](#cite_note-122) violates international law,[[120]](#cite_note-123) has the intent or effect to pre-empt final status negotiations,[[121]](#cite_note-124) and severely restricts Palestinian livelihoods, particularly limiting their freedom of movement within and from the West Bank thereby undermining their economy.[[122]](#cite_note-125)

## Status

Main article: [Status of territories occupied by Israel in 1967](/wiki/Status_of_territories_occupied_by_Israel_in_1967 "Status of territories occupied by Israel in 1967")

### Israeli occupation

Main articles: [Israeli occupation of the West Bank](/wiki/Israeli_occupation_of_the_West_Bank "Israeli occupation of the West Bank") and [Legality of the Israeli occupation of Palestine](/wiki/Legality_of_the_Israeli_occupation_of_Palestine "Legality of the Israeli occupation of Palestine")

See also: [Israeli apartheid](/wiki/Israeli_apartheid "Israeli apartheid"), [ICJ case on Israel's occupation of the Palestinian territories](/wiki/ICJ_case_on_Israel%27s_occupation_of_the_Palestinian_territories "ICJ case on Israel's occupation of the Palestinian territories"), and [Judea and Samaria Area](/wiki/Judea_and_Samaria_Area "Judea and Samaria Area")

[![Map comparing the borders of the 1947 partition plan and the armistice of 1949.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/1947-UN-Partition-Plan-1949-Armistice-Comparison.svg/330px-1947-UN-Partition-Plan-1949-Armistice-Comparison.svg.png)](/wiki/File:1947-UN-Partition-Plan-1949-Armistice-Comparison.svg)
