Planned [*Corpus separatum*](/wiki/Corpus_separatum_(Jerusalem) "Corpus separatum (Jerusalem)") with the intention that [Jerusalem](/wiki/Jerusalem "Jerusalem") would be neither Jewish nor Arab

---

**Armistice Demarcation Lines of 1949 ([Green Line](/wiki/Green_Line_(Israel) "Green Line (Israel)")):**  
  

Israeli controlled territory from 1949

[Egyptian](/wiki/Occupation_of_the_Gaza_Strip_by_the_United_Arab_Republic "Occupation of the Gaza Strip by the United Arab Republic") and [Jordanian](/wiki/Jordanian_annexation_of_the_West_Bank "Jordanian annexation of the West Bank") controlled territory from 1948 until 1967

From 1517 to 1917 the West Bank was part of the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire"). Turkey, successor state to the Ottoman Empire, [renounced](/wiki/Treaty_of_Lausanne_(1923) "Treaty of Lausanne (1923)") its territorial claims in 1923, signing the [Treaty of Lausanne](/wiki/Treaty_of_Lausanne_(1923) "Treaty of Lausanne (1923)"), and the area now called the West Bank became an integral part of the British Mandate for Palestine. During the Mandate period Britain had no right of sovereignty, which was held by the people under the mandate.[[124]](#cite_note-JQ2005-127) Nevertheless, Britain, as custodians of the land, implemented the land tenure laws in Palestine, which it had inherited from the Ottoman Turks (as defined in the [Ottoman Land Code of 1858](/wiki/Ottoman_Land_Code_of_1858#The_Ottoman_Land_Code_(as_used_in_Palestine) "Ottoman Land Code of 1858")), applying these laws to both Arab and Jewish legal tenants or otherwise.[[125]](#cite_note-128) In 1947 the UN General Assembly recommended that the area that became the West Bank become part of a future Arab state, but this proposal was opposed by the Arab states at the time. In 1948, Jordan occupied the West Bank and [annexed it in 1950](/wiki/Jordanian_annexation_of_the_West_Bank "Jordanian annexation of the West Bank").[[29]](#cite_note-digicoll.library.wisc.edu-31)

In 1967, Israel captured the West Bank from Jordan in the Six-Day War. [UN Security Council Resolution 242](/wiki/UN_Security_Council_Resolution_242 "UN Security Council Resolution 242") followed, calling for withdrawal (return to the 1949 armistice lines) from territories occupied in the conflict in exchange for peace and mutual recognition. Since 1979, the [United Nations Security Council](/wiki/United_Nations_Security_Council "United Nations Security Council"),[[126]](#cite_note-129) the [United Nations General Assembly](/wiki/United_Nations_General_Assembly "United Nations General Assembly"),[[44]](#cite_note-UNGeneva_Convention-47) the United States,[[127]](#cite_note-130) the EU,[[128]](#cite_note-131) the [International Court of Justice](/wiki/International_Court_of_Justice "International Court of Justice"),[[129]](#cite_note-132) and the [International Committee of the Red Cross](/wiki/International_Committee_of_the_Red_Cross "International Committee of the Red Cross")[[45]](#cite_note-ICRCGeneva_Convention-48) refer to the West Bank, including East Jerusalem, as occupied Palestinian territory or the occupied territories. General Assembly resolution 58/292 (17 May 2004) affirmed that the Palestinian people have the right to sovereignty over the area.[[130]](#cite_note-133)

The [International Court of Justice](/wiki/International_Court_of_Justice "International Court of Justice") (ICJ) and the [Supreme Court of Israel](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_Israel "Supreme Court of Israel") have ruled that the status of the West Bank is that of military occupation.[[131]](#cite_note-FD-134) In its 2004 advisory opinion the International Court of Justice concluded that:

> The territories situated between the Green Line and the former eastern boundary of Palestine under the Mandate were occupied by Israel in 1967 during the armed conflict between Israel and Jordan. Under customary international law, the Court observes, these were therefore occupied territories in which Israel had the status of occupying Power. Subsequent events in these territories have done nothing to alter this situation. The Court concludes that all these territories (including East Jerusalem) remain occupied territories and that Israel has continued to have the status of occupying Power.[[131]](#cite_note-FD-134)[[132]](#cite_note-ICJ2004-135)

In the same vein the Israeli Supreme Court stated in the 2004 *Beit Sourik* case that:

> The general point of departure of all parties – which is also our point of departure – is that Israel holds the area in belligerent occupation (occupatio bellica)......The authority of the military commander flows from the provisions of public international law regarding belligerent occupation. These rules are established principally in the Regulations Concerning the Laws and Customs of War on Land, The Hague, 18 October 1907 [hereinafter – the Hague Regulations]. These regulations reflect customary international law. The military commander's authority is also anchored in IV Geneva Convention Relative to the Protection of Civilian Persons in Time of War 1949.[[131]](#cite_note-FD-134)[[133]](#cite_note-BietSourik-136)

The executive branch of the Israeli government, through the Ministry of Foreign Affairs, has defined the West Bank as "disputed" instead of "occupied" territory, whose status can only be determined through negotiations. The Ministry argues that the West Bank was not captured in war because it was not under the legitimate [sovereignty](/wiki/Sovereign_state "Sovereign state") of any state prior to the Six-Day War (when it was occupied by Israel).[[46]](#cite_note-GovILDisputed_Territories-49)

The [International Court of Justice](/wiki/International_Court_of_Justice "International Court of Justice") advisory opinion of 9 July 2004, however, found that the West Bank, including East Jerusalem, is territory held by Israel under military occupation, regardless of its status prior to it coming under Israeli occupation, and that the Fourth Geneva convention applies *de jure*.[[134]](#cite_note-137) The international community regards the West Bank (including East Jerusalem) as territories occupied by Israel.[[135]](#cite_note-138)

In 2024, the ICJ reaffirmed in an [advisory opinion](/wiki/Advisory_opinion "Advisory opinion") that Israel's military occupation of the West Bank (along with the Gaza strip and East Jerusalem) is unlawful. The opinion added that Israel's "legislation and measures violate the international prohibition on [racial segregation](/wiki/Racial_segregation "Racial segregation") and [apartheid](/wiki/Israeli_apartheid "Israeli apartheid")."[[16]](#cite_note-:1-18)

International law (Article 49 of the [Fourth Geneva Convention](/wiki/Fourth_Geneva_Convention "Fourth Geneva Convention")) prohibits "transfers of the population of an occupying power to occupied territories", incurring a responsibility on the part of Israel's government to not settle Israeli citizens in the West Bank.[[136]](#cite_note-139)

As of June 2024, 146 (75.6%) of the 193 member states of the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") have [recognised](/wiki/International_recognition_of_Palestine "International recognition of Palestine") the [State of Palestine](/wiki/State_of_Palestine "State of Palestine")[[137]](#cite_note-140) within the [Palestinian territories](/wiki/Palestinian_territories "Palestinian territories"), which are recognized by Israel to constitute a single territorial unit,[[138]](#cite_note-141)[[139]](#cite_note-142) and of which the West Bank is the core of the would-be state.[[140]](#cite_note-143)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9c/Jericho_from_above.jpg/330px-Jericho_from_above.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jericho_from_above.jpg)

City of [Jericho](/wiki/Jericho "Jericho"), West Bank

### Statehood

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/Bush_abbas_presidential_guard.jpg/330px-Bush_abbas_presidential_guard.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bush_abbas_presidential_guard.jpg)

U.S. President [George Bush](/wiki/George_W._Bush "George W. Bush") and [Mahmoud Abbas](/wiki/Mahmoud_Abbas "Mahmoud Abbas") in [Ramallah](/wiki/Ramallah "Ramallah"), 2008

The future status of the West Bank, together with the [Gaza Strip](/wiki/Gaza_Strip "Gaza Strip") on the Mediterranean shore, has been the subject of negotiation between the Palestinians and Israelis, although the 2002 [Road Map for Peace](/wiki/Road_Map_for_Peace "Road Map for Peace"), proposed by the "[Quartet](/wiki/Quartet_on_the_Middle_East "Quartet on the Middle East")" comprising the United States, Russia, the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union"), and the United Nations, envisages an independent Palestinian state in these territories living side by side with [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel") (see also [history of the State of Palestine](/wiki/History_of_the_State_of_Palestine "History of the State of Palestine")). However, the "Road Map" states that in the first phase, Palestinians must end all attacks on Israel, whereas Israel must dismantle all outposts.

The [Palestinian Authority](/wiki/Palestinian_Authority "Palestinian Authority") believes that the West Bank ought to be a part of their sovereign nation, and that the presence of Israeli military control is a violation of their right to Palestinian Authority rule. The United Nations calls the West Bank and Gaza Strip *[Israeli-occupied territories](/wiki/Israeli-occupied_territories "Israeli-occupied territories")*. The United States State Department also refers to the territories as *occupied*.[[141]](#cite_note-144)[[142]](#cite_note-145)[[143]](#cite_note-146)

In 2005 the United States ambassador to Israel, [Daniel C. Kurtzer](/wiki/Daniel_C._Kurtzer "Daniel C. Kurtzer"), expressed U.S. support "for the retention by Israel of major Israeli population centres [in the West Bank] as an outcome of negotiations",[[144]](#cite_note-147) reflecting [President Bush](/wiki/George_W._Bush "George W. Bush")'s statement a year earlier that a permanent peace treaty would have to reflect "demographic realities" on the West Bank.[[145]](#cite_note-148) In May 2011 US President Barack Obama officially stated US support for a future Palestinian state based on borders prior to the 1967 War, allowing for land swaps where they are mutually agreeable between the two sides. Obama was the first US president to formally support the policy, but he stated that it had been one long held by the US in its Middle East negotiations.[[146]](#cite_note-CNNObama-149)[[147]](#cite_note-HzObama-150)

In December 2016, [a resolution](/wiki/United_Nations_Security_Council_Resolution_2334 "United Nations Security Council Resolution 2334") was adopted by [United Nations Security Council](/wiki/United_Nations_Security_Council "United Nations Security Council") that condemned Israel's settlement activity as a "flagrant violation" of international law with "no legal validity". It demands that Israel stop such activity and fulfil its obligations as an occupying power under the [Fourth Geneva Convention](/wiki/Fourth_Geneva_Convention "Fourth Geneva Convention").[[148]](#cite_note-resolution_text-151)[[149]](#cite_note-BBC-152) The United States abstained from the vote.[[150]](#cite_note-powers_statement-153)[[149]](#cite_note-BBC-152)

In 2020, President [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump") unveiled a [peace plan](/wiki/2020_Trump_Israel%E2%80%93Palestine_plan "2020 Trump Israel–Palestine plan"), radically different from previous peace plans. The plan failed to gain support.[[151]](#cite_note-154)[[152]](#cite_note-155)

West Bank was ranked 10th most electoral [democracy in the Middle East and North Africa](/wiki/Democracy_in_the_Middle_East_and_North_Africa "Democracy in the Middle East and North Africa") according to [V-Dem Democracy indices](/wiki/V-Dem_Democracy_indices "V-Dem Democracy indices") in 2024 with a score of 0.254 out of one.[[153]](#cite_note-report-156)

### Public opinion

Palestinian public opinion opposes Israeli military and settler presence in the West Bank as a violation of their right to statehood and sovereignty.[[154]](#cite_note-157) Israeli opinion is split into a number of views:

* Complete or partial withdrawal from the West Bank in hopes of peaceful co-existence in separate states (sometimes called the "[land for peace](/wiki/Land_for_peace "Land for peace")" position); (In a 2003 poll, 76% of Israelis supported a peace agreement based on that principle).[[155]](#cite_note-158)
* Maintenance of a military presence in the West Bank to reduce [Palestinian terrorism](/wiki/Palestinian_terrorism "Palestinian terrorism") by deterrence or by armed intervention, while relinquishing some degree of political control;
* [Annexation](/wiki/Annexation "Annexation") of the West Bank while considering the Palestinian population with Palestinian Authority citizenship with Israeli residence permit as per the [Elon Peace Plan](/wiki/Elon_Peace_Plan "Elon Peace Plan");
* Annexation of the West Bank and assimilation of the Palestinian population to fully fledged Israeli citizens;
* [Transfer](/wiki/Population_transfer "Population transfer") of the East Jerusalem Palestinian population (a 2002 poll at the height of the [Al-Aqsa Intifada](/wiki/Al-Aqsa_Intifada "Al-Aqsa Intifada") found 46% of Israelis favouring Palestinian transfer of Jerusalem residents).[[156]](#cite_note-159)

## Economy

Main article: [Economy of Palestine](/wiki/Economy_of_Palestine "Economy of Palestine")

As of the early-21st century, the economy of Palestine is chronically depressed, with [unemployment](/wiki/Unemployment "Unemployment") rates constantly over 20% since 2000. The unemployment rate was 19% in the West Bank in first half of 2013.[[157]](#cite_note-AUS2922p2a-160)

### Early economic impact

The Jordanians neglected to invest much in the area during their time governing the area, although there was some investment in the immediate vicinity of Jerusalem.

Soon after the 1967 war, [Yigal Allon](/wiki/Yigal_Allon "Yigal Allon") produced the [Allon Plan](/wiki/Allon_Plan "Allon Plan"), which would have annexed a strip along the Jordan River valley and excluded areas closer to the pre-1967 border, which had a high density of Palestinians. [Moshe Dayan](/wiki/Moshe_Dayan "Moshe Dayan") proposed a plan which [Gershom Gorenberg](/wiki/Gershom_Gorenberg "Gershom Gorenberg") likens to a "photo negative of Allon's."[[d]](#cite_note-162) The Allon plan evolved over a period of time to include more territory. The final draft dating from 1970 would have annexed about half of the West Bank.[[159]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeinWeizman200212–13-163) Israel had no overall approach for integrating the West Bank.[[e]](#cite_note-165)

The early occupation set severe limits on public investment and comprehensive development programmes in the territories. British and Arab commercial banks operating in the West Bank were closed down soon after Israel assumed power there. [Bank Leumi](/wiki/Bank_Leumi "Bank Leumi") then opened nine branches, without successfully replacing the earlier system. Farmers could get loans, but Palestinian businessmen avoided taking out loans from them, since they charged 9% compared to 5% interest in Jordan.[[161]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECohen1985245-166)[[162]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVan_Arkadie1977112–113-167) By June 1967, only a third of West Bank land had been registered under Jordan's *Settlement of Disputes over Land and Water Law*. In 1968, Israel moved to cancel the possibility of registering one's title with the Jordanian Land Register.[[163]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENicolettiHearne201214-168)

[Ian Lustick](/wiki/Ian_Lustick "Ian Lustick") states that Israel "virtually prevented" Palestinian investment in local industry and agriculture.[[164]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELustick201811-169) At the same time, Israel encouraged Arab labour to enter into Israel's economy, and regarded them as a new, expanded, and protected market for Israeli exports. Limited export of Palestinian goods to Israel was allowed.[[165]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVan_Arkadie1977104,_111-170) Expropriation of prime agricultural land in an economy where two thirds of the workforce had farmed is believed to account for the flight of labourers to work in Israel.[[166]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKadri1998518-171)

As much as 40% of the workforce commuted to Israel on a daily basis finding only poorly paid menial employment.[[167]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBergman2018309-172) Remittances from labourers earning a wage in Israel were the major factor in Palestinian economic growth during the 1969–73 boom years.[[168]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVan_Arkadie1977110-173) The migration of workers from the territories had a negative impact on local industry, by creating an internal labour scarcity in the West Bank and consequent pressure for higher wages there.[[169]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEVan_Arkadie1977110–111-174) The contrast between the quality of their lives and Israelis' growing prosperity stoked resentment.[[167]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBergman2018309-172)

Attempting to impose governmental authority, Israel established a licensing system according to which no industrial plant could be built without obtaining a prior Israeli permit. With Military Order No. 393 (14 June 1970), the local commander was given the power and authority to block any construction if, in his evaluation, the building might pose a danger to Israel's security. The overall effect was to obstruct manufacturing development and subordinate any local industrial activity to the exigencies of Israel's economy, or to block the creation of industries that might compete with Israel's. For example, entrepreneurs were denied a permit for a cement factory in Hebron. In order to protect Israeli farmers, melon production was forbidden, imports of grapes and dates were banned, and limits were set to how many cucumbers and tomatoes could be produced.[[170]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQuigley2005186-175) Israeli milk producers exerted pressure on the [Ministry for Industry and Trade](/wiki/Ministry_of_Economy_(Israel) "Ministry of Economy (Israel)") to stop the establishment of a competitive dairy in [Ramallah](/wiki/Ramallah "Ramallah").[[171]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEl-FarraMacMillen2000161-176)

The sum effect after two decades was that 15% of all Palestinian firms in the West Bank and Gaza employing over eight people, and 32% with seven or less, were prohibited from selling their products in Israel.[[172]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEl-FarraMacMillen2000164-177) Israeli [protectionist policies](/wiki/Protectionism "Protectionism") distorted wider trade relations to the point that, by 1996, 90% of all West Bank imports came from Israel, with consumers paying more than they would for comparable products had they been able to exercise commercial autonomy.[[173]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKadri1998517–518-178)

### Consequences of occupation

#### Economic consequences

According to a 2013 World Bank report, Israeli restrictions hinder Palestinian economic development in Area C of the West Bank.[[174]](#cite_note-AUS2922p2b-179) A 2013 [World Bank](/wiki/World_Bank "World Bank") report calculates that, if the [Interim Agreement](/wiki/Interim_Agreement_on_the_West_Bank_and_the_Gaza_Strip "Interim Agreement on the West Bank and the Gaza Strip") was respected and restrictions lifted, a few key industries alone would produce US$2.2 billion per annum more (or 23% of 2011 Palestinian GDP) and reduce by some US$800 million (50%) the Palestinian Authority's deficit; the employment would increase by 35%.[[175]](#cite_note-AUS2922pviii-180)

#### Water supply

Main article: [Water supply and sanitation in Palestine](/wiki/Water_supply_and_sanitation_in_Palestine "Water supply and sanitation in Palestine")

Amnesty International has criticized the way that the Israeli state is dealing with the regional water resources:

> Palestinians in the Occupied Palestinian Territories (OPT) do not have access to adequate, safe water supplies ... Discriminatory Israeli policies in the OPT are the root cause of the striking disparity in access to water between Palestinians and Israelis ... The inequality is even more pronounced between Palestinian communities and unlawful Israeli settlements, established in the OPT in violation of international law. Swimming pools, well-watered lawns and large irrigated farms in Israeli settlements in the OPT stand in stark contrast next to Palestinian villages whose inhabitants struggle even to meet their essential domestic water needs. In parts of the West Bank, Israeli settlers use up to 20 times more water per capita than neighbouring Palestinian communities, who survive on barely 20 litres of water per capita a day—the minimum amount recommended by the WHO for emergency situations response.[[176]](#cite_note-181)

Israeli settlers in the West Bank have seized dozens of [wells](/wiki/Well "Well") from Palestinians. The wells are privately owned by Palestinians and the settlers forcibly took them, gave them Hebrew names and, with the assistance of the Israeli military, prevent Arab people, including the wells' owners, from using the wells and the pools the wells feed.[[177]](#cite_note-182)

#### Israeli garbage disposal

Israel ratified the international [Basel Convention](/wiki/Basel_Convention "Basel Convention") treaty on Israel on 14 December 1994, according to which, any transfer of waste must be performed with an awareness of the dangers posed to the disempowered occupied people. It forbids the creation among them of "environmental [sacrifice zones](/wiki/Sacrifice_zone "Sacrifice zone")".[[178]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAloni201716-183) Israel, it is argued, uses the West Bank as a "sacrifice" zone by placing 15 waste treatment plants, which are there under less stringent rules that those required in Israel because a different legal system[*[which?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Avoid_weasel_words "Wikipedia:Avoid weasel words")*] has been organized regarding hazardous materials that can be noxious to local people and the environment. The military authorities do not make public the details of these operations. These materials include sewage sludge, infectious medical waste, used oils, solvents, metals, [electronic waste](/wiki/Electronic_waste "Electronic waste"), and batteries.[[179]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAloni20175–6-184)

In 2007 it was estimated that 38% (35 mcm a year) of all wastewater flowing into the West Bank derived from settlements and Jerusalem.[[180]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHareuveni20097-185) Of the 121 settlements surveyed, 81 had wastewater treatment plant, much of it inadequate or subject to breakdown, with much sewage flowing into lowland streams and terrain where Palestinian villages are located. Only 4 of 53 indictments for waste pollution were made over the years from 2000 to 2008,[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] whereas in Israel the laws are strictly applied and, in 2006 alone, 230 enforcements for the same abuse were enforced.[[181]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHareuveni20098–12-186) At the same time 90–95% of Palestinian wastewater was not treated, with only 1 of 4 Israeli plants built in the 1970s to that purpose functioning, and the neglect to improve the infrastructure is attributed to Israeli budgetary problems.[[182]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHareuveni200919–21-187)

After the Oslo Accords, the global community earmarked $250,000,000 for West Bank wastewater infrastructure. Israel at times insisted its approval was conditional on linking the grid to Israeli settlements, which neither the donors nor Palestinians accepted. Most of the infrastructure was subsequently destroyed by IDF military operations.[[183]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeizman2012273,n.11-188) The PA raised funds from Germany for 15 plants, but only managed to build one, at [al-Bireh](/wiki/Al-Bireh "Al-Bireh"), within Area B, though even there Israel insisted the plant process waste from the settlement of [Psagot](/wiki/Psagot "Psagot"), though refusing to pay fees for the treatment.[[182]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHareuveni200919–21-187) Palestinian town [Salfit](/wiki/Salfit "Salfit") has been deeply affected by sewage overflow channelled past the town from the settlement of Ariel.[[184]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAshly2017-189)[[185]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeizman2012273,n.14-190)

Unlike the data available for sewage treatment within Israel, the Israeli Water Commission refuses to provide public reports on 15 million cubic metres of sewage flowing from Israeli settlements in the West Bank. It claims 75% is treated adequately but independent Israeli studies (2000) suggest that only 6% met Israeli treatment standards, while 48% was either not treated adequastely or discharged raw. Since then some improvements have been implemented.[[186]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETagarKeinenBromberg2007419–420-191)

The landfill near [Al-Jiftlik](/wiki/Al-Jiftlik "Al-Jiftlik") in the [Jericho Governorate](/wiki/Jericho_Governorate "Jericho Governorate"), built on [absentee Palestinian property](/wiki/Israeli_land_and_property_laws "Israeli land and property laws") without planning or an environment impact analysis, is for the exclusive use of waste, 1,000 tons per day, produced by Israeli settlements and cities within Israel.[[187]](#cite_note-HRW_2016-192) Palestinians are restricted to 3 landfills, and permits for more have been denied unless the sites can be used to dump settlement garbage. Even if a permit is given without this agreement, settler waste under military escort is still dumped there.[[187]](#cite_note-HRW_2016-192)

Israel has been accused of engaging in 'warfare ecology' in the West Bank.[[188]](#cite_note-193) In response to [local opposition](/wiki/NIMBY "NIMBY") in Israel to waste treatment plants and the high cost of meeting stringent environment laws in that country. It has been argued that Israel had used the area of the West Bank as a 'sacrifice zone' where its waste can be dumped."[[f]](#cite_note-195)

Many [waste treatment](/wiki/Waste_treatment "Waste treatment") facilities in the West Bank were built for processing waste generated inside Israeli sovereign territory, according to [B'Tselem](/wiki/B%27Tselem "B'Tselem"), Israel's leading [human rights organization](/wiki/Human_rights_organization "Human rights organization") for monitoring the West Bank.[[190]](#cite_note-auto-196)[[191]](#cite_note-auto1-197)[[192]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAloni201716-18-198)

The Israel government requires no reporting by these West Bank facilities of the amount of waste they process or the risks they pose to the local population, and applies less rigorous regulatory standards to these facilities than it does to [solid waste](/wiki/Solid_waste "Solid waste") treatment facilities in Israel.[[193]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAloni20175-6-199) The [UNEP](/wiki/United_Nations_Environment_Programme "United Nations Environment Programme") has described Israeli vehicle dismantling in the Palestinian territories as "unregulated".[[194]](#cite_note-200) B'Tselem has said that "any transfer of waste to the West Bank is a breach of [international law](/wiki/International_law "International law") which Israel is dutybound to uphold" because according to international law "an occupied territory or its resources may not be used for the benefit of the [occupying power](/wiki/Occupying_power "Occupying power")'s own needs."[[189]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAloni201717-194)[[195]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEfrat20068-201)[[196]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWeizman201220-202)

Experts have also warned that some of these facilities are garbage dumps that endanger the purity of the mountain [aquifer](/wiki/Aquifer "Aquifer"), which is one of the largest sources of water in the region.[[195]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEfrat20068-201) In addition to the exporting of Israeli waste and the waste generated by settlers, Israeli industrial and manufacturing operations with harmful health effects are typically outsourced to the West Bank where they can be carried out with little to no regulatory oversight.[[193]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAloni20175-6-199)[[197]](#cite_note-203)

#### Palestinian water and sewage

Main article: [Water supply and sanitation in Palestine](/wiki/Water_supply_and_sanitation_in_Palestine "Water supply and sanitation in Palestine")

In 1995, the Palestinian Water Authority (PWA) was established by a presidential decree. One year later, its functions, objectives, and responsibilities were defined through a by-law, giving the PWA the mandate to manage water resources and execute the water policy.[[198]](#cite_note-husseini-204)

About 90% of the Palestinians in the Territories had access to [improved sanitation](/wiki/Improved_sanitation "Improved sanitation") in 2008.[[199]](#cite_note-JMP-205) [Cesspits](/wiki/Cesspit "Cesspit") were used by 39% of households, while access to the sewer network increased to 55% in 2011, up from 39% in 1999.[[200]](#cite_note-PCBS_11-206) In the West Bank, only 13,000 out of 85,000 m3 of wastewater were treated in five municipal wastewater treatment plants in [Hebron](/wiki/Hebron "Hebron"), [Jenin](/wiki/Jenin "Jenin"), [Ramallah](/wiki/Ramallah "Ramallah"), [Tulkarem](/wiki/Tulkarem "Tulkarem"), and [Al-Bireh](/wiki/Al-Bireh "Al-Bireh").[[201]](#cite_note-Fatta-207) The Al Bireh plant was constructed in 2000 with funding by the [German](/wiki/Germany "Germany") [aid agency](/wiki/Aid_agency "Aid agency") [KfW](/wiki/KfW "KfW").[[202]](#cite_note-208) According to the World Bank report, the other four plants perform poorly concerning efficiency and quality.[[203]](#cite_note-209)

#### Resource extraction

Based on the number of quarries per km2 in Areas A and B, it is calculated that, were Israel to lift restrictions, a further 275 quarries could be opened in Area C. The World Bank estimates that Israel's virtual ban on issuing Palestinians permits for quarries there costs the Palestinian economy at least US$241 million per year.[[204]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENiksicEddinCali201458-210)
In international law drawing on the [Hague Conventions](/wiki/Hague_Conventions_of_1899_and_1907 "Hague Conventions of 1899 and 1907") (Article 55), it is established that an occupying power may reap some value from the resources of the country occupied but not deplete its assets, and that the [usufruct](/wiki/Usufruct "Usufruct") must benefit the people under occupation. The Oslo Accords agreed to hand over mining rights to the Palestinian Authority. Israel licenses eleven settlement quarries in the West Bank and they sell 94% of their material to Israel, which arguably constitutes "depletion" and pays royalties to its West Bank military government and settlement municipalities.[[187]](#cite_note-HRW_2016-192)

Thus the German [cement firm quarrying at Nahal Raba](/wiki/HeidelbergCement "HeidelbergCement") paid out €430,000 ($479,000) in taxes to the [Samaria Regional Council](/wiki/Samaria_Regional_Council "Samaria Regional Council") in 2014 alone.[[187]](#cite_note-HRW_2016-192) The Israeli High Court rejected a petition that such quarrying was a violation by stating that after four decades Israeli law must adapt to "the realities on the ground". The state did undertake not to open more quarries.[[205]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGross2011-211)

A Human Rights Watch report contrasts the difference between a Palestinian-owned quarry company in [Beit Fajar](/wiki/Beit_Fajar "Beit Fajar") and that of a European one working on what Israeli considers its state land. The European company obtained a concession and licence to harvest stone, whereas Israel refuses permits for most of the roughly 40 Beit Fajar quarries, or nearly any other Palestinian-owned quarry in the West Bank under Israeli administration.[[187]](#cite_note-HRW_2016-192)

Israel had denied Palestinians permits to process minerals in that area of the West Bank.[[204]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENiksicEddinCali201458-210) The products of the Israeli cosmetics firm [Ahava](/wiki/Ahava "Ahava"), established in 1988, were developed in laboratories at the West Bank [Dead Sea](/wiki/Dead_Sea "Dead Sea") settlements of [Mitzpe Shalem](/wiki/Mitzpe_Shalem "Mitzpe Shalem") and [Kalya](/wiki/Kalya "Kalya"). 60% of their production is sold in the EU market.[[206]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENicolettiHearne201221–22-212) In 2018 The UN, stating that the violations were both "pervasive and devastating" to the local Palestinian population, identified some 206 companies which do business with Israeli settlements in the West Bank.[[207]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENebehay2018-213)

Roughly 73 percent of global [bromine](/wiki/Bromine "Bromine") production comes from Israeli and Jordanian exploitation of the Dead Sea. The potential incremental value that could accrue to the Palestinian economy from the production and sales of [potash](/wiki/Potash "Potash"), bromine, and magnesium has been conservatively estimated at US$918 million per annum, or 9 percent of GDP.[[208]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWorld_Bank201312–13-214)[[209]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeckouche2017156-215) The lost earnings from not being allowed to process Dead Sea minerals such as potash, and for making bromide-based flame retardants, based on calculations of comparable use by Israel and Jordan, suggest a figure of $642 million.[[210]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENiksicEddinCali201458–60-216)

#### Loss of cultural property

Albert Glock argued that Israel is responsible for the disappearance of significant parts of the Palestinian cultural patrimony, by confiscating Arab cultural resources. In 1967 it appropriated the Palestine Archaeological Museum[[g]](#cite_note-218) and its library in East Jerusalem.[[h]](#cite_note-220) Often these losses are personal, as when homes are ransacked and looted of their valuables. The journalist Hamdi Faraj, jailed for endangering public order, had his 500-volume library confiscated, including copies of the Bible and Qur'an and, when he applied for their restitution, was told all the books had been accidentally burnt.[[213]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFriedman198396-221)
The Israeli occupation has wrought a profound change in Palestinian identity, which clings to a sense of a "paradise lost" before the changes brought out by the 1967 conquest.[[214]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKamrava2016116-222)[[i]](#cite_note-224)

#### Tourism

Main article: [Tourism in Palestine](/wiki/Tourism_in_Palestine "Tourism in Palestine")

Palestine contains several of the most significant sites for Muslims, Christians, and Jews, and are endowed with a world-class heritage highly attractive to tourists and pilgrims.[[216]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWorld_Bank201320-225) The West Bank Palestinians themselves have difficulties in accessing the territory for recreation.

Based on 1967 figures, the Palestinian [Dead Sea](/wiki/Dead_Sea "Dead Sea") Coastline is roughly 40 km in length, of which 15% (6 kilometres) could lend itself to the same tourist infrastructure developed by Jordan and Israel in their respective areas. Were Israel to permit a parallel development of this Palestinian sector, the World Bank estimates that 2,900 jobs would be added, allowing the Palestinian economy a potential value-added input of something like $126 million annually.[[217]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENiksicEddinCali201465–66-226) It is also the only maritime recreational outlet for West Bankers, but according to an [Acri](/wiki/Association_for_Civil_Rights_in_Israel "Association for Civil Rights in Israel") complaint to the Israeli Supreme Court in 2008 Palestinians are often barred or turned away from the beaches at their only access point, the [Beit Ha'arava checkpoint](/wiki/Beit_HaArava "Beit HaArava") on [Route 90](/wiki/Highway_90_(Israel%E2%80%93Palestine) "Highway 90 (Israel–Palestine)"). Acri claimed the ban responds to fears by settlers who operate tourist concessions in this West Bank area that they will lose Jewish customers if there are too many West Bank Palestinians on the beaches.[[j]](#cite_note-228)

The key Palestinian towns in the West Bank for tourism are East Jerusalem, Bethlehem, and Jericho. All access points are controlled by Israel and the road system, checkpoints and obstacles in place for visitors desiring to visit Palestinian towns leaves their hotels half-empty.[[219]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIsaac2013147-229) From 92 to 94 cents in every dollar of the tourist trade goes to Israel.[[220]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIsaac2013144-230) The general itineraries under Israeli management focus predominantly on Jewish history. Obstacles placed in the way of Palestinian-managed tourism down to 1995 included withholding licences from tour guides, and hotels, for construction or renovation, and control of airports and highways, enabling Israel to develop a virtual monopoly on tourism.[[220]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIsaac2013144-230)

### Transportation and communications

#### Road system

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a3/Road_5066_A.JPG/330px-Road_5066_A.JPG)](/wiki/File:Road_5066_A.JPG)

A road in the West Bank

Main article: [Palestinian freedom of movement § Restriction of movement in the West Bank](/wiki/Palestinian_freedom_of_movement#Restriction_of_movement_in_the_West_Bank "Palestinian freedom of movement")

In 2010, the West Bank and Gaza Strip together had 4,686 km (2,912 mi) of roadways.[[8]](#cite_note-CIA-10)

It has been said that for "Jewish settlers, roads *connect*; for Palestinians, they *separate*."[[221]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKamrava201686-231) Between 1994 and 1997, the Israeli Defense Forces (IDF) built 290 km (180 mi) of bypass roads in the territories, on appropriated land because they ran close to Palestinian villages.[[222]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEfrat200685-232) The given aim was said to be to afford protection to settlers from Palestinian sniping, bombing, and drive-by shootings.[[223]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGalchinsky2004117-233) For [TAU](/wiki/Tel_Aviv_University "Tel Aviv University") emeritus professor of geography Elisha Efrat, they ignored the historical topography, road systems, and environmental characteristics of the West Bank, and simply formed an apartheid network of "octopus arms which hold a grip on Palestinian population centres".[[k]](#cite_note-234)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/39/Bethlehem.JPG/330px-Bethlehem.JPG)](/wiki/File:Bethlehem.JPG)

Roads of [Bethlehem](/wiki/Bethlehem "Bethlehem"), West Bank

A large number of embankments, concrete slabs, and barriers impeded movement on primary and secondary roads. The result was to [cantonize](/wiki/Canton_(country_subdivision) "Canton (country subdivision)") and fragment Palestinian townships, and cause endless obstacles to Palestinians going to work, schools, markets, and relatives.[[l]](#cite_note-236) Ramallah was cut off from all of its feeder villages in 2000.[[224]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHass20026-235)

Though prohibited by law, confiscation of Palestinian identity cards at checkpoints is a daily occurrence. At best drivers must wait for several hours for them to be returned, when, as can happen, the IDs themselves are lost as soldiers change shifts, in which case Palestinians are directed to some regional office the next day, and more checkpoints to get there.[[222]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEfrat200685-232) Even before the Al Aqsa Intifada, [UNFPA](/wiki/United_Nations_Population_Fund "United Nations Population Fund") estimated that 20% of pregnant West Bank women were unable to access [prenatal care](/wiki/Prenatal_care "Prenatal care") because of the difficulties and delays caused by crossing checkpoints, and dozens were forced to deliver their children on the roadside.[[225]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAswad2007-237)

Constant uncertainty and the inability to plan are the results for Palestinians of the Israeli military rules governing their movements. The [World Bank](/wiki/World_Bank "World Bank") noted that additional costs arising from longer travelling caused by restrictions on movement through three major routes in the West Bank alone ran to (2013) USD 185 million a year, adding that other, earlier calculations (2007) suggest restrictions on the Palestinian labour market cost the West Bank approximately US$229 million per annum. It concluded that such imposed restrictions had a major negative impact on the local economy, hindering stability and growth.[[226]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEWorld_Bank201330-238) In 2007, official Israeli statistics indicated that there were 180,000 Palestinians on Israel's secret travel ban list. 561 roadblocks and checkpoints were in place (October), the number of Palestinians licensed to drive private cars was 46,166 and the annual cost of permits was $454.[[227]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMakdisi201063–64-239)

These checkpoints, together with the separation wall and the restricted networks restructure the West Bank into "land cells", freezing the flow of normal everyday Palestinian lives.[[228]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHandel2010259,_261-240) Israel sets up [flying checkpoints](/wiki/Random_checkpoint "Random checkpoint") without notice. In 2017, 2,941 flying checkpoints were set up along West Bank roads, around 327 a month. There were 476 unstaffed physical obstacles (dirt mounds, concrete blocks, gates, and fenced sections) on roads. Of the gates erected at village entrances, 59 were always closed.[[229]](#cite_note-241)

The checkpoint system did not ease up after the Oslo Accords, but was strengthened after them, which has been interpreted as suggesting their function is to assert control over Palestinians, and as a sign of an unwillingness to yield ground in the West Bank.[[230]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEfrat200686-242) According to [PA Health Ministry statistics](/wiki/Health_Minister_of_the_Palestinian_National_Authority "Health Minister of the Palestinian National Authority") relating to the period from 2000 to 2006, of 68 Palestinian women who gave birth to their children while held up at checkpoints, 35 miscarried and 5 died while delivering their child there.[[231]](#cite_note-243) [Machsom Watch](/wiki/Machsom_Watch "Machsom Watch") accumulated over a mere five years (2001–2006) some 10,000 eyewitness reports and testimonies regarding the difficulties faced by Palestinians trying to negotiate West Bank checkpoints.[[232]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeshet2013viii-244)

Transportation infrastructure is particularly problematic as Palestinian use of roads in Area C is highly restricted, and travel times can be inordinate; the Palestinian Authority has also been unable to develop roads, airports or railways in or through Area C,[[233]](#cite_note-245) while many other roads were restricted only to public transportation and to Palestinians who have special permits from Israeli authorities.[[234]](#cite_note-humanitarianinfo2-246)[[235]](#cite_note-247)[[236]](#cite_note-248)

At certain times, Israel maintained more than 600 checkpoints or roadblocks in the region.[[237]](#cite_note-249)
Movement restrictions on main roads between cities were still (as of 2005) blamed for poverty and economic depression in the West Bank.[[238]](#cite_note-250) Underpasses and bridges, 28 of which have been constructed and 16 of which are planned, link Palestinian areas separated from each other by Israeli settlements and bypass roads"[[239]](#cite_note-humanitarianinfo1-251)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/Jericho_checkpoint_2005.jpg/330px-Jericho_checkpoint_2005.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jericho_checkpoint_2005.jpg)

A checkpoint before entering [Jericho](/wiki/Jericho "Jericho"), 2005

Israeli restrictions were tightened in 2007.[[240]](#cite_note-252)

[Route 4370](/wiki/Route_4370 "Route 4370"), which has a concrete wall dividing the two sides, one designated for Israeli vehicles, the other for Palestinian. The wall is designed to allow Palestinians to pass north–south through Israeli-held land and facilitate the building of additional Jewish settlements in the Jerusalem neighbourhood.[[241]](#cite_note-253)

#### Rail

As of February 2012[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=West_Bank&action=edit), a plan for 475-kilometre rail network, establishing 11 new rail lines in the West Bank, was confirmed by Israeli Transportation Ministry. The West Bank network would include one line running through Jenin, Nablus, Ramallah, Jerusalem, Ma'aleh Adumim, Bethlehem, and Hebron. Another would provide service along the Jordanian border from Eilat to the Dead Sea, Jericho, and Beit She'an and from there toward Haifa in the west and also in a northeasterly direction. The proposed scheme also calls for shorter routes, such as between Nablus and Tul Karm in the West Bank, and from Ramallah to the Allenby Bridge crossing into Jordan.[[242]](#cite_note-254)

#### Airports

The only airport in the West Bank is the [Atarot Airport](/wiki/Atarot_Airport "Atarot Airport") near [Ramallah](/wiki/Ramallah "Ramallah"), but it has been closed since 2001.

#### Crossing points to Jordan and Egypt

The [Allenby Bridge](/wiki/Allenby_Bridge "Allenby Bridge"), or 'King Hussein Bridge', is the main port for the Palestinians in the West Bank to cross the Jordanian borders. This crossing point is controlled by Israel since 1967. It was inaugurated on 11 December 2011 under the military order "175" entitled 'An order concerning transition station'. Later, Order '446' was issued which annexed the [Damia Bridge](/wiki/Damia_Bridge "Damia Bridge") crossing point to the Allenby Bridge as a commercial crossing point only. Goods were exported to Jordan, while the import was banned for security purposes.[[243]](#cite_note-Euro-Mid-255)

In 1993, the Palestinian National Authority, according to Oslo Accord assigned by PLO and the Israeli government, became a partial supervisor over the [Rafah Border Crossing](/wiki/Rafah_Border_Crossing "Rafah Border Crossing") to Gaza Strip. The Palestinian Authority was responsible for issuing passports to Palestinians in the West Bank and Gaza Strip. Israel remained the major responsible party for this crossing point. According to the agreement, Israel has the right to independently inspect luggage and to maintain security. In addition, it can prevent anyone from using the crossing.[[243]](#cite_note-Euro-Mid-255)[[244]](#cite_note-256)

#### Telecommunication

Main article: [Communications in Palestine](/wiki/Communications_in_Palestine "Communications in Palestine")

The Palestinian [Paltel](/wiki/Paltel "Paltel") telecommunication companies provide communication services such as [landline](/wiki/Landline "Landline"), [cellular network](/wiki/Cellular_network "Cellular network"), and Internet in the West Bank and [Gaza Strip](/wiki/Gaza_Strip "Gaza Strip"). Dialling code [+970](/wiki/%2B970 "+970") is used in the West Bank and all over the Palestinian territories. Until 2007, the Palestinian mobile market was monopolized by [Jawwal](/wiki/Jawwal "Jawwal"). A new [mobile operator](/wiki/Mobile_operator "Mobile operator") for the territories launched in 2009 under the name of [Wataniya Telecom](/wiki/Wataniya_Telecom "Wataniya Telecom"). The number of Internet users increased from 35,000 in 2000 to 356,000 in 2010.[[245]](#cite_note-257)

## Demographics

Main article: [Demographics of Palestine](/wiki/Demographics_of_Palestine "Demographics of Palestine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/35/Nablus_Children_Victor_Grigas_2011_-1-84.jpg/330px-Nablus_Children_Victor_Grigas_2011_-1-84.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nablus_Children_Victor_Grigas_2011_-1-84.jpg)

Palestinian girl in [Nablus](/wiki/Nablus "Nablus")

In December 2007, an official census conducted by the Palestinian Authority found that the [Palestinian Arab](/wiki/Palestinian_Arab "Palestinian Arab") population of the West Bank, including [East Jerusalem](/wiki/East_Jerusalem "East Jerusalem"), was 2,345,000.[[8]](#cite_note-CIA-10)[[246]](#cite_note-258) However, the [World Bank](/wiki/World_Bank "World Bank") and American-Israeli Demographic Research Group identified a 32% discrepancy between first-grade enrollment statistics documented by the Palestinian Ministry of Education and Palestinian Central Bureau of Statistics (PCBS)' 2007 projections,[[247]](#cite_note-259) with questions also raised about the PCBS' growth assumptions for the period 1997–2003.[[248]](#cite_note-260) The [Israeli Civil Administration](/wiki/Israeli_Civil_Administration "Israeli Civil Administration") put the number of Palestinians in the West Bank at 2,657,029 as of May 2012.[[249]](#cite_note-261)[[250]](#cite_note-262)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/17/Tal_Menashe_-_students_in_the_garden.JPG/330px-Tal_Menashe_-_students_in_the_garden.JPG)](/wiki/File:Tal_Menashe_-_students_in_the_garden.JPG)

Jewish children in [Tal Menashe](/wiki/Tal_Menashe "Tal Menashe").

In 2014, there were 389,250 [Israeli settlers](/wiki/Israeli_settlement "Israeli settlement") living in the West Bank, excluding East Jerusalem,[[251]](#cite_note-263) as well as around 375,000 living in Israeli-annexed East Jerusalem. There are also small ethnic groups, such as the [Samaritans](/wiki/Samaritans "Samaritans") living in and around [Nablus](/wiki/Nablus "Nablus"), numbering in the hundreds.[[252]](#cite_note-tsu-264)

As of October 2007, around 23,000 Palestinians in the West Bank worked in Israel every day, while another 9,200 worked in Israeli settlements. In addition, around 10,000 Palestinian traders from the West Bank were allowed to travel every day into Israel.[[253]](#cite_note-265) By 2014, 92,000 Palestinians worked in Israel legally or illegally, twice as many as in 2010.[[254]](#cite_note-266)

In 2008, approximately 30% of Palestinians or 754,263 persons living in the West Bank were [refugees](/wiki/Palestinian_refugees "Palestinian refugees") or descendants of refugees from villages and towns located in what became Israel during the [1948 Arab–Israeli War](/wiki/1948_Arab%E2%80%93Israeli_War "1948 Arab–Israeli War"), according to [UNRWA](/wiki/UNRWA "UNRWA") statistics.[[255]](#cite_note-267)[[256]](#cite_note-268)[[257]](#cite_note-269) A 2011 EU report titled "Area C and Palestinian State Building" reported that before the Israeli occupation in 1967, between 200,000 and 320,000 Palestinians used to live in the Jordan Valley, 90% which is in Area C, but demolition of Palestinian homes and prevention of new buildings has seen the number drop to 56,000, 70% of which live in Area A, in Jericho.[[258]](#cite_note-EUobserver_13_January_2012-270)[[259]](#cite_note-h20120112-271)[[260]](#cite_note-jps-272) In a similar period, the Jewish population in Area C has grown from 1,200 to 310,000.[[258]](#cite_note-EUobserver_13_January_2012-270)

The most densely populated part of the region is a mountainous spine, running north–south, where the cities of [Jerusalem](/wiki/Jerusalem "Jerusalem"), [Nablus](/wiki/Nablus "Nablus"), [Ramallah](/wiki/Ramallah "Ramallah"), [al-Bireh](/wiki/Al-Bireh "Al-Bireh"), [Jenin](/wiki/Jenin "Jenin"), [Bethlehem](/wiki/Bethlehem "Bethlehem"), [Hebron](/wiki/Hebron "Hebron"), and [Yattah](/wiki/Yattah "Yattah") are located as well as the [Israeli settlements](/wiki/Israeli_settlements "Israeli settlements") of [Ariel](/wiki/Ariel_(city) "Ariel (city)"), [Ma'ale Adumim](/wiki/Ma%27ale_Adumim "Ma'ale Adumim"), and [Beitar Illit](/wiki/Beitar_Illit "Beitar Illit"). Ramallah, although relatively mid in population compared to other major cities as [Hebron](/wiki/Hebron "Hebron"), [Nablus](/wiki/Nablus "Nablus"), and [Jenin](/wiki/Jenin "Jenin"), serves as an economic and political centre for the Palestinians. Near Ramallah the new city of [Rawabi](/wiki/Rawabi "Rawabi") is under construction.[[264]](#cite_note-276)[[265]](#cite_note-277) [Jenin](/wiki/Jenin "Jenin") in the extreme north and is the capital of north of the West Bank and is on the southern edge of the [Jezreel Valley](/wiki/Jezreel_Valley "Jezreel Valley"). [Modi'in Illit](/wiki/Modi%27in_Illit "Modi'in Illit"), [Qalqilyah](/wiki/Qalqilyah "Qalqilyah"), and [Tulkarm](/wiki/Tulkarm "Tulkarm") are in the low foothills adjacent to the [Israeli Coastal Plain](/wiki/Israeli_Coastal_Plain "Israeli Coastal Plain"), and [Jericho](/wiki/Jericho "Jericho") and [Tubas](/wiki/Tubas_(city) "Tubas (city)") are situated in the [Jordan Valley](/wiki/Jordan_Valley "Jordan Valley"), north of the [Dead Sea](/wiki/Dead_Sea "Dead Sea").

### Ethnic cleansing

As part of "[Operation Iron Wall](/wiki/Operation_Iron_Wall "Operation Iron Wall")" Israel forcibly removed 32,000 Palestinians from their homes in West Bank [refugee camps](/wiki/Palestinian_refugee_camps "Palestinian refugee camps") in [Tulkarem](/wiki/Tulkarem "Tulkarem"), [Nur Shams](/wiki/Nur_Shams "Nur Shams"), and  [Jenin](/wiki/Jenin_refugee_camp "Jenin refugee camp") in January and February 2025, according to a detailed investigation by [Human Rights Watch](/wiki/Human_Rights_Watch "Human Rights Watch"). According to the report, these forced expulsions were the largest since the major expulsion of Palestinians in 1967, and constitute [ethnic cleansing](/wiki/Ethnic_cleansing "Ethnic cleansing"), [war crimes](/wiki/War_crimes "War crimes"), and [crimes against humanity](/wiki/Crimes_against_humanity "Crimes against humanity").[[266]](#cite_note-278)[[267]](#cite_note-279)

### Religion

Main article: [Palestinians § Religion](/wiki/Palestinians#Religion "Palestinians")

The population of the West Bank is 80–85% Muslim (mostly Sunni) and 12–14% Jewish. The remainder are 1–2.5% Christian (mostly Greek Orthodox), and others.[[8]](#cite_note-CIA-10)

### Education

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/27/Wikipedia_education_program_birzeit_university_2016_ceremoney_15.jpg/250px-Wikipedia_education_program_birzeit_university_2016_ceremoney_15.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wikipedia_education_program_birzeit_university_2016_ceremoney_15.jpg)

Palestinian students at [Birzeit University](/wiki/Birzeit_University "Birzeit University") in 2016

Seven universities are operating in the West Bank:

* [Bethlehem University](/wiki/Bethlehem_University "Bethlehem University"), a [Roman Catholic](/wiki/Roman_Catholic "Roman Catholic") institution of the [Lasallian](/wiki/Brothers_of_the_Christian_Schools "Brothers of the Christian Schools") tradition partially funded by the [Vatican](/wiki/Holy_See "Holy See"),[[268]](#cite_note-280) opened its doors in 1973.[[269]](#cite_note-281)
* In 1975, Birzeit College (located in the town of [Bir Zeit](/wiki/Bir_Zeit "Bir Zeit") north of [Ramallah](/wiki/Ramallah "Ramallah")) became [Birzeit University](/wiki/Birzeit_University "Birzeit University") after adding third- and fourth-year college-level programmes.[[270]](#cite_note-282)
* An-Najah College in [Nablus](/wiki/Nablus "Nablus") likewise became [An-Najah National University](/wiki/An-Najah_National_University "An-Najah National University") in 1977.[[271]](#cite_note-283)
* [Hebron University](/wiki/Hebron_University "Hebron University") was established as College of Shari'a in 1971 and became Hebron University in 1980.[[272]](#cite_note-284)
* [Al-Quds University](/wiki/Al-Quds_University "Al-Quds University") was founded in 1995, unifying several colleges and faculties in and around East Jerusalem.[[273]](#cite_note-285)
* In 2000, the [Arab American University](/wiki/Arab_American_University "Arab American University") – the only private university in the West Bank – was founded outside of [Zababdeh](/wiki/Zababdeh "Zababdeh"), with the purpose of providing courses according to the [American system of education](/wiki/Education_in_the_United_States "Education in the United States").[[274]](#cite_note-286)
* [Ariel University](/wiki/Ariel_University "Ariel University") is located in the [Israeli settlement](/wiki/Israeli_settlement "Israeli settlement") of [Ariel](/wiki/Ariel_(city) "Ariel (city)") and was granted full university status on 17 July 2012.[[275]](#cite_note-287) It was established in 1982.

Most universities in the West Bank have politically active student bodies, and elections of student council officers are normally along party affiliations. Although the establishment of the universities was initially allowed by the Israeli authorities, some were sporadically ordered closed by the Israeli Civil Administration during the 1970s and 1980s to prevent political activities and violence against the [IDF](/wiki/Israel_Defense_Forces "Israel Defense Forces"). Some universities remained closed by military order for extended periods during years immediately preceding and following the first Palestinian Intifada, but have largely remained open since the signing of the Oslo Accords despite the advent of the Al-Aqsa Intifada (Second Intifada) in 2000.

The founding of Palestinian universities has greatly increased education levels among the population in the West Bank. According to a Birzeit University study, the percentage of Palestinians choosing local universities as opposed to foreign institutions has been steadily increasing; as of 1997, 41% of Palestinians with bachelor's degrees had obtained them from Palestinian institutions.[[276]](#cite_note-288) According to UNESCO, Palestinians are one of the most highly educated groups in the Middle East "despite often difficult circumstances".[[277]](#cite_note-289) The literacy rate among Palestinians in the West Bank and the Gaza Strip according to the [Palestinian Central Bureau of Statistics](/wiki/Palestinian_Central_Bureau_of_Statistics "Palestinian Central Bureau of Statistics") (PCBS) is 94.6% for 2009.[[278]](#cite_note-290)

## Culture

Main article: [Culture of Palestine](/wiki/Culture_of_Palestine "Culture of Palestine")

### Television and radio

The [Palestinian Broadcasting Corporation](/wiki/Palestinian_Broadcasting_Corporation "Palestinian Broadcasting Corporation") broadcasts from an AM station in Ramallah on 675 kHz; numerous local privately owned stations are also in operation. Most Palestinian households have a radio and TV, and satellite dishes for receiving international coverage are widespread. PalTel began implementing an initiative to provide ADSL broadband internet service to all households and businesses.

Israel's cable television company [Hot](/wiki/Hot_(Israel) "Hot (Israel)"), satellite television provider [Yes](/wiki/Yes_(company) "Yes (company)"), AM/FM radio broadcast stations, and public television stations all also operate in the West Bank. Broadband internet service by Bezeq's ADSL and by the cable company is available as well. The Al-Aqsa Voice broadcasts from Dabas Mall in [Tulkarem](/wiki/Tulkarem "Tulkarem") at 106.7 FM. The [Al-Aqsa TV](/wiki/Al-Aqsa_TV "Al-Aqsa TV") station shares these offices.

174. **[^](#cite_ref-AUS2922p2b_179-0)** [World Bank 2013](#CITEREFWorld_Bank2013), p. 2. "While internal Palestinian political divisions have contributed to investor aversion to the Palestinian territories, Israeli restrictions on trade, movement and access are clearly the binding constraint to investment: these restrictions substantially increase the cost of trade and make it impossible to import many production inputs into the Palestinian territories, as illustrated, for instance, on the example of the telecommunications sector. For Gaza, the restrictions on import and export are in particular severe. In addition to the restrictions on labour movement between the Palestinian territories, the restrictions on movement of labour within the West Bank have been shown to have a strong impact on employability, wages, and economic growth. Israeli restrictions render much economic activity very difficult or impossible to conduct on about 61 percent of the West Bank territory, called Area C. Restrictions on movement and access, and the stunted potential of Area C."
175. **[^](#cite_ref-AUS2922pviii_180-0)** [World Bank 2013](#CITEREFWorld_Bank2013), p. viii. "[...] assumed that the various physical, legal, regulatory and bureaucratic constraints that currently prevent investors from obtaining construction permits, and accessing land and water resources are lifted, as envisaged under the Interim Agreement. [...] It is understood that realizing the full potential of such investments requires other changes as well – first, the rolling back of the movement and access restrictions in force outside Area C, which prevent the easy export of Palestinian products and inhibit tourists and investors from accessing Area C; and second, further reforms by the Palestinian Authority to better enable potential investors to register businesses, enforce contracts, and acquire finance. [...] Neglecting indirect positive effects, we estimate that the potential additional output from the sectors evaluated in this report alone would amount to at least USD 2.2 billion per annum in valued added terms – a sum equivalent to 23 percent of 2011 Palestinian GDP. The bulk of this would come from agriculture and Dead Sea minerals exploitation. [...] x. Tapping this potential output could dramatically improve the PA's fiscal position. Even without any improvements in the efficiency of tax collection, at the current rate of tax/GDP of 20 percent the additional tax revenues associated with such an increase in GDP would amount to some USD 800 million. Assuming that expenditures remain at the same level, this extra resource would notionally cut the fiscal deficit by half – significantly reducing the need for donor recurrent budget support. This major improvement in fiscal sustainability would in turn generate significant positive reputational benefits for the PA and would considerably enhance investor confidence. xi. The impact on Palestinian livelihoods would be impressive. An increase in GDP equivalent to 35 percent would be expected to create substantial employment, sufficient to put a significant dent in the currently high rate of unemployment. If an earlier estimated one-to-one relationship between growth and employment was to hold, this increase in GDP would lead to a 35 percent increase in employment. This level of growth in employment would also put a large dent in poverty, as recent estimates show that unemployed Palestinians are twice as likely to be poor as their employed counterparts."
